The syllabus is approved for use in England, Wales and Northern Ireland as a Cambridge International Level 1/Level Gomez is the manager of a small computer repair business. Table 1 shows some costs and prices for his business. As it is a competitive market, Gomez knows it is important to maintain customer loyalty and revenue. Gomez knows that a lower break-even point would help improve profits. He thinks the best way to lower the break-even point is to raise prices. Table 1: Cost and price information Price per customer $

question_text: Certificate. 2 0450/11/M/J/15 Country S is enjoying an economic boom. During this time, businesses have increased their profits and unemployment has fallen. The only problem is inflation. All Active is a private limited company. It owns 20 sport and fitness clubs in country S. The Managing Director said: â€˜This is a good time to expand by opening 5 more clubs. Finding the right locations is important. We will need to recruit more employees.â€™ He cannot decide whether it would be an advantage for All Active to become a public limited company. (a) Identify two stages of the recruitment process. Stage 1: Stage 2: [2] (b) What is meant by an â€˜economic boomâ€™? [2] (c) Explain how falling unemployment might affect All Active. [4] 5 0450/11/M/J/15 [Turn over (d) Identify and explain two factors that All Active should consider when deciding on the location of the new clubs. Factor 1: Explanation: Factor 2: Explanation: [6] (e) Consider the advantages and disadvantages for All Active of being converted to a public limited company. Do you think All Active should become a public limited company? Justify your answer. [6] 6 0450/11/M/J/15

subject: Business Studies
question_text: Si and Ramon are friends. They want to set up a photography business. They would take pictures at weddings, birthdays and other special events. The business will be a partnership. Based on their primary market research, Si believes there is enough demand. Ramon has produced a business plan which shows they need $8000 for a shop and $4000 for equipment. They plan to spend $500 on promotion, but cannot agree on the best method to use. (a) What is meant by a â€˜partnershipâ€™? [2] (b) Identify two methods of primary market research that Si and Ramon might have used. Method 1: Method 2: [2] (c) Identify and explain two possible sources of finance that they could use to obtain the equipment. Source 1: Explanation: Source 2: Explanation: [4] 7 0450/11/M/J/15 [Turn over (d) Identify and explain two advantages to Si and Ramon of having a business plan. Advantage 1: Explanation: Advantage 2: Explanation: [6] (e) Consider two methods of promotion that would be suitable for Si and Ramon to use. Which of these two methods would be the most appropriate? Justify your answer. [6] 8 0450/11/M/J/15
mark_scheme_text: 3 (a) What is meant by a â€˜partnershipâ€™? [2] Clear understanding [2] â€“ 2 [or more] people who agree to own and run a business together [2] Some understanding [1] â€“ e.g. 2 or more people run a business Do not accept features (b) Identify two methods of primary market research that Si and Ramon might have used. [2] Application [2 Ã— 1] â€“ award 1 mark for each relevant method Points could include: questionnaire/survey, interview, consumer panel, observation, focus group Note: Do not accept field research/internet â€“ too vague Page 6 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge IGCSE â€“ May/June 2015 0450 11 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2015 (c) Identify and explain two possible sources of finance that they could use to obtain the equipment. [4] Knowledge [2 Ã— 1] â€“ identification of relevant sources [max 2] Application [2 Ã— 1] â€“ award 1 mark for each relevant explanation Relevant points might include: â€¢ Bank loan [k] so can spread cost of cameras over a period of time [app] â€¢ Savings [k] so no need to pay interest on the $4000 [app] â€¢ Borrow from family and friends [k] â€¢ Leasing [k] as there may not be enough demand [app] â€¢ Hire purchase [k] so their new business does not have to pay a large one off cost [app] â€¢ Government grant [k] â€¢ Additional partner [k] Do not accept retained profits or overdraft as this is a new business Application marks may be awarded for appropriate use of the following: new business, $4000, partnership, cameras or examples of relevant equipment [not equipment on its own], business plan (d) Identify and explain two advantages to Si and Ramon of having a business plan. [6] Knowledge [2 Ã— 1] â€“ award 1 mark for each relevant advantage [max 2] Application [2 Ã— 1] â€“ award 1 mark if relevant reference to Si and Ramonâ€™s business Analysis [2 Ã— 1] â€“ award 1 mark for each relevant explanation Relevant points might include: â€¢ Support loan applications [k] as bank/lenders will want to know that the $12 500 [app] can be repaid [an] â€¢ Clear aims/guidance for business [k] so they know what they have to do to get there [an] â€¢ help decision making [k] as Si believe there is customer demand for service before start [app] so will not waste time and money targeting the wrong people [an] â€¢ helps understand the possible risks [k] as they might not have run a business before [app] â€¢ provides an estimate of costs [k] for the photography business [app] so they will know how much they need [an] â€¢ provides a checklist [k] as they set up [app] so that they donâ€™t forget any important task [an] Application marks may be awarded for appropriate use of the following: $12 500/$8000/$4000, $500 photography business, new business, set up Page 7 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge IGCSE â€“ May/June 2015 0450 11 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2015 (e) Consider two methods of promotion that would be suitable for Si and Ramon to use. Which of these two methods would be most appropriate? Justify your answer. [6] Knowledge [1] â€“ award 1 mark for identification

question_text: Javid is a company that manufactures a range of luxury chocolates using batch production. Over the past year, profit has fallen due to increased competition . In order to cut costs, the Finance Director has suggested the company introduces new technology into the production process. The Human Resources Director is not sure that workers would benefit from the introduction of new technology. (a) What is meant by â€˜batch productionâ€™? [2] (b) Identify two advantages (other than cutting costs) to the company of using new technology. Advantage 1: Advantage 2: [2] (c) Identify and explain two reasons why the quality of chocolates might be important to this company. Reason 1: Explanation: Reason 2: Explanation: [4] 9 0450/11/M/J/15 (d) Identify and explain two problems that a fall in profit might cause for the company. Problem 1: Explanation: Problem 2: Explanation: [6] (e) Do you think that the introduction of new technology would benefit Javidâ€™s workers? Justify your answer. question_text: Certificate. 2 0450/12/M/J/15 Alkin is a multinational company. It manufactures breakfast food products including many leading brands of cereal. Alkin plans to start selling in country Z, which is a fast-growing market. Alkin plans to form a joint venture with a local business, based in country Z. â€˜Effective communication will be important to our success but I think there are many advantages in expanding this wayâ€™ said Alkinâ€™s Managing Director. The Government of country Z is worried about the effect of multinational companies like Alkin on consumers . (a) What is meant by a â€˜multinational companyâ€™? [2] (b) What is meant by a â€˜brandâ€™? [2] (c) Identify and explain two reasons why effective communication might be important for Alkin. Reason 1: Explanation: Reason 2: Explanation: [4] 7 0450/12/M/J/15 [Turn over (d) Identify and explain two advantages to Alkin of using a joint venture to expand into country Z. mark_scheme_text: 2 (a) What is meant by a â€˜multinational company?â€™ [2] Clear Understanding [2] â€“ a business with factories, production or service operations in more than one country OR a business with production or service operation in another country outside its normal area of operation Some Understanding [1] â€“ e.g. has businesses everywhere / works or operates in more than one country Do not accept â€˜sells in more than one countryâ€™ as could equally apply to exporter Do not accept examples (b) What is meant by a â€˜brandâ€™? [2] Clear Understanding [2]: (unique) name, feature or design / logo or image of a product or business [1] that distinguishes it from other products or businesses [1] Some Understanding [1]: e.g. something which makes a product â€˜stand outâ€™ [1] Do not accept examples Page 4 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge IGCSE â€“ May/June 2015 0450 12 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2015 (c) Identify and explain two reasons why effective communication might be important for Alkin. question_text: Mabel wants to set up her own hairdressing business. She will need to build good customer relationships. She has done some secondary market research to find out possible demand and what methods of promotion to use. She has decided to buy her inventory (stock) from a wholesaler. Mabel is now looking for the right location for her business. She thinks that location will be more important than price charged to the success of her business. (a) Identify two ways that a business could build good customer relationships. Way 1: Way 2: [2] (b) What is meant by â€˜secondary market researchâ€™? [2] (c) Identify and explain two methods of promotion (other than promotional pricing) that Mabel might use. mark_scheme_text: 3 (a) The company Balance Sheet helps David assess the performance of the business. (i) Calculate the current ratio and acid test ratio for 2014. [4] (ii) Compare your two ratio results with those of 2013 (see Appendix 2) [4] Knowledge [2 Ã— 1] â€“ award one mark for each correct ratio Analysis [2 Ã— 1] â€“ award one mark for each correct calculation (i) Current ratio 1000/600 = 1.66/ 1.67 (1K + 1Ap) Accept 1300/600 = 2.16/ 2.17 if working shown Acid test ratio 700/600 = 1.16/ 1.17 (1K + 1Ap) Accept 1000/600 = 1.6/ 1.67 if working shown 2 marks for each correct answer; 1 mark if correct method/formula but incorrect answer (ii) Application + Analysis [2 Ã— 2] â€“ award one application mark for the comparison between the figures and one analysis mark for each explanation of the calculation result from (i) Indicative response: The current ratio is lower [ap] because the current liabilities have increased [an]. The acid test ratio is higher (ap) because the amount of inventories has fallen so less working capital is being tied up in inventories [an]. NB do not reward â€˜betterâ€™ or â€˜worseâ€™ for the comparison of the figures but an explanation of why the figure is better or worse can be credited. Page 8 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge IGCSE â€“ May/June 2015 0450 21 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2015 (b) David wants to increase efficiency in his factory. Consider Option A and Option B and then recommend which one he should choose. Justify your answer. mark_scheme_text: 1 (a) Samah and Selina wrote a business plan before FF started trading 5 years ago. Identify and explain two ways their business plan might have helped to make the business successful. [8] Knowledge [2 Ã— 1] â€“ award one mark for each relevant way Analysis [2 Ã— 1] â€“ award one mark for a relevant explanation for each way Application [2 Ã— 2] â€“ award two application marks for each way Award one mark for each relevant way (maximum of two), such as: â€¢ It helps to secure a bank loan or overdraft â€¢ The entrepreneur thinks ahead and plans out carefully the next few years â€¢ Reduces risks of failure as the owners have thought through problems the business might have â€¢ To clarify the aims and objectives / targets to aim for â€¢ Aids financial planning Award a maximum of three additional marks for each explanation â€“ 2 of which must be applied to this context - of the way a business plan might help make the business successful. Indicative response: A business plan reduces risk for the business (k) as Samah and Selina will have thought through problems such as where to source their ingredients for the ice cream and what it will cost them (ap). This will allow them to prepare for any problems and stop these causing difficulties for the business (an). This might include preparing for cash flow problems at times of the year when demand is low (ap). Possible application marks: partnership; produces ice cream; high quality ingredients; expensive ingredients; ice cream sold for a high price; borrow finance easily from the bank; sales increased for four years; demand high for 6 months; cost / revenue data from appendix 3; new products / flavours; redundant workers; have taken out a bank loan; high income consumers. [12] Relevant points might include: Advantages Full-time workers Likely to be better trained More committed to the business More likely to be trained / skilled than part-time workers Feel secure in job â€“ more motivated (although several made redundant) Part-time workers More flexible hours Easier to extend hours at busy times Accept lower pay as fits in with family commitments Knowledge / Analysis / Evaluation â€“ award up to 10 marks using the levels-based mark scheme below. Level 2 4â€“6 marks Detailed discussion of advantages of part-time and / or full-time workers. Level 1 1â€“3

[4] Knowledge [2 Ã— 1] â€“ award 1 mark for each relevant advantage [max 2] Application [2 Ã— 1] â€“ award 1 mark for each relevant explanation in context of TWH Relevant points might include: â€¢ some variety in workersâ€™ jobs [k] leading to better motivation [app] â€¢ allows more variety of products to be made [k] so can respond to changes in demand for different toys [app] â€¢ if machinery breaks down other work can continue [k] so could improve efficiency [app] â€¢ flexible way of working [k] â€¢ economies of scale/bulk buying [k] so lower average costs [app] â€¢ More output than job production [k] Application marks may be awarded for appropriate use of the following: relevant use of numbers, toys, motivation, average cost, efficiency, new technology Do not accept cheaper, faster, more output (if just this is stated) Page 3 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge IGCSE â€“ October/November 2015 0450 11 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2015 (d) Identify and explain two ways that TWH could improve the motivation of its employees. [6] Knowledge [2 Ã— 1] â€“ identification of relevant way [max 2] Application [2 Ã— 1] â€“ award 1 mark for each way if relevant reference made to TWH Analysis [2 Ã— 1] â€“ award 1 mark for each relevant explanation Relevant points might include: â€¢ Job rotation [k] swapping between making different toys [app] they are not bored doing the same thing [an] â€¢ training [k] on the new technology [app] so employees feel valued [an] â€¢ better communication with management [k] so workers feel that they are important to the business [an] so efficiency improves [app] â€¢ improve pay [k] they pay $6 [app] as pay $.50 less per hour [an] OR bonuses/piece rate [k] so make more toys [app] to get paid above their usual wage [an] â€¢ improved working conditions/environment [k] Note: other options acceptable e.g. fringe benefits (allow only once), job enrichment, promotion Do not accept work harder alone as analysis because a reason for this must be given Application marks may be awarded for appropriate use of the following: relevant use of numbers, toys, average costs, efficiency, new technology. question_text: Certificate. 2 0450/11/O/N/15 Ramford is a private limited company. It has four factories which make a wide range of food products including breakfast cereal and ice cream. The Finance Director is worried about the companyâ€™s financial performance. She has read that many businesses failed in the last year. â€˜I think we are suffering from diseconomies of scaleâ€™ she said. Ramfordâ€™s directors have decided to cut the number of different products it makes from 80 to 10. Table 2: Extract from accounts 2013 2014 Revenue (sales) ($m) 200 150 (Net) profit ($m) 70
mark_scheme_text: 2 (a) What is meant by a â€˜private limited companyâ€™? [2] Clear understanding [2] e.g. a business whose shares cannot be sold to the general public [2] Or shares only sold to family and friends [2] Some understanding [1] e.g. outlines general features of limited companies e.g. have limited liability/separate legal identity from owners/can sell shares/incorporated Do not accept in private sector/no Government involvement as generic statement also applies to unlimited businesses. (b) Identify two causes of business failure. [2] Knowledge [2 Ã— 1] 1 mark per cause Points might include: â€¢ lack of management skills â€¢ actions of competition â€¢ change in government laws â€¢ poor product range/outdated products â€¢ lack of demand/recession â€¢ lack of/poor market research â€¢ lack of finance/cash flow problems â€¢ diseconomies of scale/over expansion Note: points could refer to either new or established business. Accept any reasonable answer. (c) Identify and explain two diseconomies of scale that Ramford might experience. [4] Knowledge [2 Ã— 1] â€“ award 1 mark for each relevant diseconomy [max 2] Application [2 Ã— 1] â€“ award 1 mark for each relevant explanation in context of Ramford Relevant points might include: â€¢ Poor communication [k] as messages need to be sent/received from 4 factories [app] â€¢ Low morale/motivation [k] as worried products they make might be cut [app] â€¢ Slow decision making [k] as 80 products to consider [app] â€¢ Lack of control [k] Application marks may be awarded for appropriate use of the following: calculations/use of numbers, factories, 80 products, reduced profit, financial performance, wide range, private limited company. Page 5 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge IGCSE â€“ October/November 2015 0450 11 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2015 (d) Identify and explain two factors that Ramford should consider in deciding which products to remove from its product range. [6] Knowledge [2 Ã— 1] â€“ identification of relevant factor [max 2] Application [2 Ã— 1] â€“ award 1 mark for each way if relevant reference made to this business Analysis [2 Ã— 1] â€“ award 1 mark for each relevant explanation Relevant points might include: â€¢ Stage in product life cycle [k] if some foods are in decline [app] few people are likely to be buying them anyway [an] â€¢ Availability of substitutes/competition [k] if many breakfast cereals [app] so might not be cost effective to keep making it [an] â€¢ Cost of producing each product [k] as worried about financial performance [app] might want to stop making expensive items [an] â€¢ Current level of sales/popularity/demand [k] as profits falling [app] need to remove the least popular lines [an] â€¢ Not suit current season [k] Application marks may be awarded for appropriate use of the following: calculations/use of numbers, food, cereal, ice cream, factories, 80 to 10 products, reduce profit, financial performance. mark_scheme_text: 1 (a) Identify and explain one advantage and one disadvantage to CC of being a public limited company. [8] Knowledge (2 Ã— 1) â€“ award one mark for each advantage/disadvantage identified Analysis (2 Ã— 1) â€“ award one mark for an explanation of each advantage/disadvantage Application (2 Ã— 2) â€“ award two application marks for each advantage/disadvantage Award one mark for each advantage/disadvantage (maximum of two), such as: Advantages: â€¢ Raise large amounts of capital through share issue â€¢ No limit to the number of shares sold â€¢ Shares can be sold to the public â€¢ Shares can be sold on the stock exchange â€¢ Limited liability â€¢ Company has separate legal identify for shareholders â€¢ Continuity of company if shareholder dies Disadvantages: â€¢ Accounts are published â€¢ Risk of takeover â€¢ Divorce between ownership and control â€¢ Loss of control as increased number of shareholders Award a maximum of three additional marks for each advantage/disadvantage â€“ 2 of which must be applied to this context . Indicative response: Shares can be sold on the stock exchange (k) and this means that CC can raise large amounts of capital to finance the building of new factories in other countries (ap). CC will not have to pay interest on this source of finance and so costs of producing camping equipment are lower (ap) as only dividends may be paid when profits are gained (an). Possible application marks: recognition that the plc is large; business wants to expand; manufactures camping equipment; customers have tents or caravans; merging with a competitor; three factories; capital intensive method of production; camping cooker; prices just below competitors prices; CC share price; takeover; equipment listed from Appendix 1. There may be other examples in context which have not been included here. Consider the advantages and disadvantages of each of the following three ways CC could use. Recommend the most effective way to use. Justify your answer. Application [2 Ã— 1] â€“ award 1 mark for each explanation in context. Relevant points might include: â€¢ Opportunities for achievement [k] â€¢ Recognition/praise [k] as would enjoy being thanked for making quality clothes [app] â€¢ Development/training [k] so that they can learn new techniques to further improve their skills [app] â€¢ Promotion opportunities [k] so have a chance to become Operations Manager [app] â€¢ Give workers more responsibilities or job enrichment [k] by allowing employee to oversee trouser production [app] â€¢ Job enlargement OR different/variety of tasks [k] e.g. Switch between cutting materials and sewing [app] â€¢ Delegation or empowerment [k] allowing workers to make decisions about choice of fabrics [app]. Do not accept hygiene factors (such as status, security, conditions, relations with workers/supervisors) as theory says these do not act as motivators. Application marks may be awarded for appropriate use of the following: 75, skilled employees, fashion clothing (or examples such as trousers, shirts), enjoy working, cost of equipment, few leave, production process or associated tasks involved in the production process such as cutting, sewing, factory. Points might include: â€¢ Cost of equipment [k] can they afford it [an] as have a low level of profits [app] â€¢ Need for retraining [k] which would increase costs [an] â€¢ Impact on motivation e.g. Demotivate workers/boring [k] as skilled [app] so many want to leave [an] â€¢ Workers might be worried about future job security [k] so may not increase efficiency [app] â€¢ Space [k] only a small factory [app] there might not be enough room [an] â€¢ Cost of maintenance [k] â€¢ Redundancy costs [k] of skilled workers [app] â€¢ Could reduce wastage/less mistakes [k].

mark_scheme_text: 4 (a) The Government has increased interest rates to reduce inflation. Identify and explain two ways an increase in interest rates might affect FF. [8] Knowledge [2 Ã— 1] award one mark for each relevant way identified Analysis [2 Ã— 1] award one mark for a relevant explanation of each way Application [2 Ã— 2] award two application marks for each relevant way Award one mark for each relevant way (maximum of two), such as: â€¢ Decreased demand â€“ as customers have increased mortgage/loan payments â€¢ Increased cost on new loans â€“ less likely to expand â€¢ Increased cost of overdraft â€“ increased expenses/reduced profit â€¢ Increased costs from higher interest repayments â€“ prices of FF services increases/reduces demand. Award a maximum of three additional marks for each explanation â€“ two of which must be applied to this context â€“ of each of these ways. Indicative response: FF will have increased costs from higher interest repayments on new loans (k) therefore price of photographs may have to rise (ap). Other guests at weddings can take photographs so the elasticity of demand may be elastic, leading to falling revenue if prices are increased (ap). NB â€“ answer should be clearly related to interest rates increasing and NOT just inflation increasing. mark_scheme_text: 2 (a) Identify and explain two reasons why P & P might respond to the pressure group and change what the company is doing. [8] Knowledge [2 Ã— 1] â€“ award one mark for each reason Analysis [2 Ã— 1] â€“ award one mark for a relevant explanation for each reason Application [2 Ã— 2] â€“ award two application marks for each reason Award one mark for each reason (maximum of two), such as: â€¢ Want to keep a good reputation for being environmentally friendly â€¢ Wants to keep customers from switching to competitors â€¢ Does not want to upset local residents â€¢ Wants to avoid fines or legal action â€¢ Wants to keep a favourable reputation for being a good employer Award a maximum of three additional marks for each explanation â€“ 2 of which must be applied to this context â€“ of why the business might respond to the pressure group and change what the company is doing. Indicative response: To not lose customers who may buy from competitors if they are against businesses polluting the environment (k). The pollution affects a lot of people such as fishermen (ap) and may mean there are a greater number of people lobbying the government for the company to reduce pollution put into the river (ap). The more people affected then the more effective the pressure group and the more likely they are to change to another company if P & P do not change the way they produce paint (an). Possible application marks: the Government of country X; paint manufacturer; large limited company; large factory next to a river; pollution; waste; dumping in the river; makes tins (cans) of paint; sells to large retailers; 150 production workers; employees live near to the factory; details of two options outlined in Appendix 2; details of pollution caused by paint production outlined in Appendix 3. (8) Knowledge [2 Ã— 1] â€“ award one mark for each relevant factor Analysis [2 Ã— 1] â€“ award one mark for a relevant explanation for each factor Application [2 Ã— 2] â€“ award two application marks for each factor Award one mark for each relevant factor (maximum of two), such as: â€¢ Size of the output/ level of demand â€¢ Various styles of product made â€¢ Easier/quicker to respond to changes in demand â€¢ Not enough capital to use capital-intensive methods Award a maximum of three additional marks for each explanation â€“ 2 of which must be applied to this context â€“ of the factor which may have influenced the decision to use batch production. Indicative response: Size of the output [k] as if a large output is not required then flow production will not be used as too many products will be produced (an). mark_scheme_text: 3 (a) Ghaziz and Jasmin plan to expand their business. Identify and explain one economy of scale and one diseconomy of scale for the business as it expands. DC (ST) 134247/1 [Turn over Cambridge International Examinations Cambridge International General Certificate of Secondary Education * 7 0 8 8 2 4 0 5 7 1 * BUSINESS STUDIES 0450/ Identify and explain two ways in which TT might achieve quality production. Way 1: Explanation: Way 2: Explanation: [8] 3 0450/21/M/J/17 [Turn over (b) Consider the advantages and the disadvantages of the two options for expansion. Recommend the best option to choose. Justify your answer. Option A: Option B: Recommendation: [12] 4 0450/21/M/J/17
mark_scheme_text:
1(a) Identify and explain two ways in which TT might achieve quality production. Knowledge [2 â‹… 1] â€“ award one mark for each way. Analysis [2 â‹… 1] â€“ award one mark for a relevant explanation for each way. Application [2 â‹… 2] â€“ award two application marks for each way. Award one mark for each way (maximum of two), from this list: Â• Quality control Â• Quality assurance Â• Quality labour â€“ skilled/trained workers Â• Quality materials â€“ consider producing own T shirts instead of buying in Â• Use new technology/new computer-controlled equipment/CAD,CAM or CIM Â• TQM/quality circles Do not accept JIT or lean production. Award a maximum of three additional marks for each explanation â€“ 2 of which must be applied to this context - of how the quality can be achieved. Indicative response: Quality control (k) means that each batch of childrenâ€™s T-shirts are checked at the end of the production process (app) If there are any faults then the batch can be sewn up and repairs made to any gaps in the seams (app) so that products only leave the factory if they are correctly made and in perfect condition (an). Possible application marks: Ready-made plain white T-shirts; high quality material; prints designs; T-shirts imported from country Q, a developing country; 50 different designs; batches production of various sizes; personalized T-shirts; buying new computer controlled equipment for $500 000; retailers expect high quality products There may be other examples in context that have not been included here. 8 0450/21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2017 Page 3 of 12 Answer Question Marks

1(b) Consider the advantages and the disadvantages of the two options for expansion. Recommend the best option to choose. Justify your answer. Relevant points might include: Advantages Disadvantages Option A â€“ take over competing company Specialised in different product range â€“ increase sales. Economies of scale possible â€“ reduce management layers â€“ reduce wage costs. Increase efficiency â€“ share good practice - lower unit costs â€“ more competitive â€“ increase profit. High cost to buy out shareholders. No guarantee of success â€“ no expertise in this market. Low staff morale â€“ job insecurity â€“ lack of coordination between the two management structures â€“ diseconomies of scale. Option B â€“ expand own production facilities Increased efficiency from new equipment â€“ increased output â€“ increased sales. Not using a wholesaler â€“ absorb profit margin. Option B is cheaper than Option A. May need joint venture/use an agent â€“ reduce profit margin â€“ lower profit. Lack of knowledge of export market â€“ culture/taste may be different â€“ more difficult to have effective marketing. Knowledge/Analysis/Evaluation â€“ award up to 10 marks using the levels-based mark scheme below. Knowledge / Analysis / Evaluation Level 3 At least 2 â‹… Level 2 + 9â€“10 marks for well justified recommendation of which option to choose and why not choose the alternative option. 7â€“8 marks for limited recommendation of whic

2(a) Identify two motivational theories and explain how TT could use these theories to help increase worker motivation Knowledge [2 â‹… 1] â€“ award one mark for each theory Analysis [2 â‹… 1] â€“ award one mark for a relevant explanation for each theory Application [2 â‹… 2] â€“ award two application marks for each theory Award one mark for each theory (maximum of two), from: Â• Taylor â€“ scientific management approach, money is the main motivator, pay according to tasks performed so speed is encouraged, specialisation would make best use of skills Â• Maslow â€“ hierarchy of needs, satisfy physiological, safety, social, self-esteem, self- actualisation needs to increase motivation Â• Herzberg â€“ two factor theory where hygiene factors relate to the working environment and motivating factors relate to improving the job itself so that work is meaningful and the worker is given responsibility Note: There may be reference to other motivational theorists. Explain why they will want to see each of these statements. mark_scheme_text:
1(a) Identify two financial statements the directors will need to see before expanding CC. Explain why they will want to see each of these statements. Knowledge [2 â‹… 1] â€“ award one mark for each financial statement Analysis [2 â‹… 1] â€“ award one mark for a relevant explanation for each financial statement Application [2 â‹… 2] â€“ award two application marks for each financial statement Award one mark for each relevant named financial statement (max 2) and one mark for explanation of why the directors will want to view these statements before expanding. Examples might include: Â• income statement/Profit and loss account â€“ to assess profitability/availability of retained profit/ need for loan/ability to repay loan Â• balance sheet/Statement of financial position â€“ assess liquidity position/ assets for security against loan/assess value of assets that could be sold Â• cash flow forecast/statement â€“ shows cash position/ability to cover expenses of expansion/liquidity position/ability to repay loans Award a maximum of three additional marks for each explanation â€“ 2 of which must be applied to this context â€“ of the financial statement. Indicative response: Balance sheet (k) to see if the business will be able to borrow $500 000 (app). The balance sheet will show the liabilities of the company and will show if the bank is likely to give CC a loan for the purchase of the new automated equipment (app). The directors will want to see if there are sufficient assets to be used as security for the loan (an). Possible application marks: Already operating in a niche market; high quality ingredients; chocolates/high quality chocolates/luxury chocolates; hand-made chocolates; factory; private limited company; set up 15 years ago in country X; labour-intensive production techniques; 100 production workers; on-the-job training for new workers; developing a new brand of chocolate bar; targeted at the mass market; purchase a new automated production line; output is expected to be high; borrow $500 000. 1(b) Consider the following three factors that affect CC when importing all of its ingredients. Which is the most important factor when deciding whether to import ingredients? Justify your answer. Relevant points might include: Exchange rate May fluctuate and this will affect the import prices of ingredients â€“ affects costs â€“ difficult to plan for â€“ may result in having much higher costs than budget. Exchange rate is already low â€“ higher cost of imported ingredients â€“ raise prices â€“ lower sales - reduce profit. If exchange rate increased/high â€“ lower cost of imported ingredients â€“ lower prices â€“ increase sales â€“ increase profit. Import tariffs Increased cost of imported materials â€“ need to raise prices of finished chocolates or reduce profit. Note: Do not award fixed quantity i.e. quota Delivery time Perish

3(a) Refer to the organisation chart in Appendix 2. Identify and explain the chain of command for BB and the span of control for Stella. Knowledge [2 â‹… 1] â€“ award one mark each for chain of command and span of control. Analysis [2 â‹… 1] â€“ award one mark each for explanation of chain of command and span of control Application [2 â‹… 2] â€“ award two application marks each for chain of command and span of control Award one mark each for chain of command and span of control (maximum of two). Award a maximum of three additional marks for each explanation â€“ 2 of which must be applied to the information in Appendix 2 â€“ of the chain of command and span of control. Chain of command is the structure in an organisation which allows instructions to be passed down from senior management to lower levels of management. (k) In this case BB has 3 levels (ap) with Stella at the top and then the next layer are supervisors and finally there are the child care workers. (ap) So in BB any instructions from Stella pass through these three layers. (an) Span of control is the number of subordinates working directly under a Manager. (k) In this case Stella has a span of control of 3. (ap) She commands the three supervisors in their work each day and this is quite a narrow span of control as it is a small business, (ap) which ensures that there is good communication. (an) Possible application marks: childrenâ€™s nursery; parents; information from Appendix 2. There may be other examples in context that have not been included here. 8 0450/23 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2017 Page 9 of 12 Question Answer Marks

1(a) Identify and explain two ways a business plan could have helped Peter when setting up Value Gym. Award one mark for each way (maximum of two), such as: Â• To help gain finance from a bank/other investors/government grants Â• To set objectives/goals/targets Â• To encourage careful planning that will reduce risks of financial failure Â• To be clear how the business is going to operate and to identify the human and physical requirements of the business Â• To identify the target market from market research/identify the level of demand for the gym Award a maximum of three additional marks for each explanation of the way â€“ 2 of which must be applied to this context. Indicative response: Peter will have written the business plan to help him gain finance. (1) Peter will have taken it to show the bank manager to help persuade him to give VG the loan needed to start the gym. (ap) The business plan will show financial forecasts to show that the business will be able to repay the loan. (1) Therefore after the bank has given Peter the loan he will have the capital he needs to set up the gym and start signing up members. 2(a) Identify and explain four reasons why making a profit is important to Peter . Award one mark for each reason (maximum of four), such as: Â• Source of finance/retained profit/Peter could use the profit for reinvestment â€“ expand the gym by buying new equipment Â• To ensure survival of the business/avoid closure Â• Reward for enterprise â€“ reward for Peterâ€™s hard work/good ideas to start the business Â• Reward for risk taking â€“ Peter risked losing his investment of $5,000 Â• Indicator of success â€“ the more profit Peter makes the more his success will be recognised by customers/employees Note: Do not reward payment of day-to-day expenses Award a maximum of one additional mark for each explanation suitable for this context. Indicative response: It is a reward for risk taking (1) as Peter has risked $5,000 of his own money in the business and could have gained interest on this money if he had put it in the bank.(1) 8

2(b) Peter is considering the following two options. Recommend which option he should choose. Justify your answer . Advantages Disadvantages Employ his own personal trainers Â• Full control over trainers Â• Higher profit especially when busy with many clients Â• Pay trainers whether or not they have clients Â• More expensive when not busy Charge a fixed fee to personal trainers who work for themselves to use VG facilities with their clients Â• Fixed amount paid to VG Â• Risk of low demand taken by the trainer Â• Less expenses as no wages to pay each month Â• Additional revenue/profit lost to gym Â• Cannot control the quality of the training provided for clients Â• Maybe unreliable and this could affect reputation of VG Level 1 [1â€“3 marks] Outline of advantages and/or disadvantages of each option. (max of 3 marks) E.g. Peter has full control over his own trainers if he employs them. 2(a) Explain the externalities of each of the following: The external cost of large amounts of rubbish left on the beach Award 1 mark for an understanding of an external cost plus 1 mark for each example of an impact on a third party where the third party is identified A maximum of 2 marks can be awarded if only examples of external costs are in the answer The answer could be one external cost developed or several costs developed with the impacts on third parties identified for a maximum of 4 marks Possible answers might include: Â• Costs paid for by the rest of society as a result of the business activity Â• The externality in this case is local people visiting the beach and leaving rubbish which then affects hotel guests and the hotel itself Â• Eye-sore spoils the view - puts off tourists so DH may lose trade Â• Increased cost of cleaning beach â€“ to hotel or government Â• Harm to wildlife and environment â€“ especially marine animals getting injured Indicative response: The rubbish makes it unpleasant for tourists and local people visiting the beach (1) and therefore may reduce demand for hotel rooms at DH. 4(a) Identify and explain two reasons why cash flow forecasting is important to Hilda. Award one mark for each relevant reason (maximum of one each), such as: Â• To know how much cash is flowing into the business and how much cash is flowing out of the business Â• To ensure the business does not run out of cash Â• To plan for times when cash might be low or negative Â• To ensure she can meet day-to-day expenses Â• To help make decisions for the future to know how much cash is available Â• To help gain a bank loan by showing the cash flow forecast to the bank manager Â• To support planning for buying the new hotel Â• Assessing how well the business is being managed by comparing the cash flow forecast with the actual cash flow Â• To help identify where costs could be reduced Â• To assess whether the business is holding too much cash which could be put to better use Award a maximum of three additional marks for the explanation of each reason â€“ 2 of which must be applied to this context . Indicative response: To ensure the business does not run out of cash (1) as the hotel is empty for two months of the year and will have no cash flowing in. (ap) This could cause a cash flow problem if the hotel is still paying out wages for the staff (ap) which could cause Hilda to run out of cash and be unable to trade if she has not planned for this to happen by taking out an overdraft (1). Possible application marks: Small hotel; 39 employees; demand for hotel accommodation is increasing; half full 6 months; full 4 months; empty 2 months; most guests are local tourists on coach tours; wants to expand by taking over a hotel; details of ABC/XYZ hotels. mark_scheme_text:
3(a) Calculate for 2017: (i) Gross Profit Margin GPM = ($500 000 â€“ $100 000) / $500 000 = 80% 2 marks for correct answer â€“ 1 mark if correct method but incorrect answer. (ii) Profit margin PM = $125 000 / $500 000 = 25% 2 marks for correct answer â€“ 1 mark if correct method but incorrect answer. (iii) Explain why these profitability ratios have changed between 2016 and 2017. Award a maximum of two additional marks for each explanation of the profitability ratios. Indicative response: The gross profit margin has improved because the cost of sales has not increased (1) in line with the increase in revenue. (1) The profit margin has stayed the same, which means that although revenue has increased, costs of sales did not increase but profit did increase. (1) However, expenses increased which kept the ratio the same. (1) 8

3(b) Consider the effects of the following three changes in legal controls on AH. Which change is likely to have the biggest impact on AH? Justify your answer. Relevant points might include: Advantages Disadvantages Heating engineers must now have health and safety qualifications Â• Fewer accidents as all staff are qualified Â• One off payment for training Â• Increased training costs Â• May need to train employees as some are part-qualified Employees are now protected against unfair dismissal Â• Motivating â€“ increased security Â• Harder to get rid of unsatisfactory employees â€“ may reduce efficiency Increase in the minimum wage Â• Higher wages â€“ happier workers â€“ increased motivation Â• Higher wage costs â€“ lower profit Â• Increased costs â€“ increased prices â€“ lower demand 12 0450/23 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2018 Page 10 of 13 Question Answer Marks

3(b) Level 1 [1â€“3 marks] 1 mark for outline of impact of each change. (max of 3 marks) E.g. Wage costs will increase. Level 2 [4â€“6 marks] Detailed discussion of the impact of each change. 1 â‹… L2 explanation can gain 4 marks and a further mark can be awarded for each additional L2 explanation (max 6 marks) E.g. 1(a) Identify and explain two advantages and two disadvantages of GT being a public limited company. Award 1 mark for each relevant advantage/disadvantage (maximum of 4), such as: Advantages: â€¢ Limited liability â€¢ Incorporated business with separate legal identify â€¢ Shares can be issued to the general public â€¢ Opportunity to raise very large sums of capital â€¢ No restrictions on buying, transferring or selling of shares â€¢ High status and easier to attract suppliers Disadvantages: â€¢ Quite complicated legal formalities â€¢ Many regulations and controls including publication of accounts/accounts can be seen by competitors â€¢ Selling shares to the public is expensive â€¢ Danger the owners may lose control if too many shares are issued/risk of takeover Award a maximum of 1 additional mark for each explanation. Indicative response: Limited liability [1] means the shareholders can only lose the capital they invested in the company and not their personal possessions. [1]. 8 0450/21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2018 Page 4 of 14 Question Answer Marks

1(b) Consider the advantages and disadvantages of the following two options for distributing GT products. Recommend the option GT should choose. Justify your answer. Relevant points might include: Advantages Disadvantages Continue selling to large shops Â• Already established channel of distribution and links with retailers Â• Easier to use in store promotions as already has relationship with retailer Â• Lower delivery costs compared to online Â• Harder to expand sales if using the same channels and not new ones Â• May sell many competing goods in shops alongside GT toys Only sell its products online Â• Sell to a wider range of customers Â• Customers can access all the range of products Â• Lower price as no retail profit margin Â• Possibly allows prices to be reduced to make the toys more competitive Â• May lose existing customers if they want to see and handle toys before buying Â• May not have a cost-effective delivery network available Level 1 (1â€“3 marks) 1 mark for each L1 statement (max of 3 marks) E.g. Lower price as no retail profit included. Level 2 (4â€“6 marks) Detailed discussion of the advantages and disadvantages of each option. 1 â‹… L2 explanation can gain 4 marks and a further mark can be awarded for each additional L2 explanation (max 6 marks) E.g. Lower price as no retail profit included so this may attract more customers leading to higher sales of toys and profit for GT. However, GT may lose existing customers if they prefer to feel and see the toys to check they are suitable for their children before buying. This may reduce the sales of toys to existing customers and result in a lowering of toy sales for GT. (4 marks for L2 answer plus 1 application mark for referring to the purchase of toys for their children). 12 0450/21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2018 Page 5 of 14 Question Answer Marks

2(a) Identify and explain two roles of the Operations manager at GT. Knowledge [2 â‹… 1] â€“ award 1 mark for each role. Analysis [2 â‹… 1] â€“ award 1 mark for a relevant explanation for each role. Application [2 â‹… 2] â€“ award 2 application marks for each role. Award 1 mark for each role (maximum of 2), such as: â€¢ Planning the production such as setting a target of an increase of 10% in output of toy production â€¢ Organising the tasks done by the production line workers such as deciding which worker will fit heads onto the figures and which ones will package the dolls â€¢ Coordinating different departments with the production department ensuring that for example the purchasing department orders the right quantity of components for the toy cars ready for their assembly â€¢ Commanding the supervisors on the production lines to ensure they all know what they should be doing to keep to their targets and meet deadlines â€¢ Controlling the employees on the production line to make sure they are all meeting their targets and producing toys efficiently. Award a maximum of 3 additional marks for each explanation of role of the Operations manager at GT â€“ 2 of which must be applied to this context. Indicative response: Planning (k) the production of toys in the GT factory. (ap) This gives a sense of direction and purpose to the operations department, as they will all have a common purpose to work towards. (an) The output of toys should therefore go more smoothly as the correct components to make the toy cars should arrive on time to be ready for assembly on the production line. (ap). Possible application marks: manufacturer of toys; toys sold to large toy shops throughout country Y; toys also exported; Marketing manager wants to sell toys online; toys manufactured using batch production; design and packaging of toys need to be adapted for each different export market; sells well designed toys of high quality; safety of the child is very important; components are imported and uses JIT; need to purchase cheaper components to reduce costs; currently low levels of worker motivation; recent price increases have resulted in falling sales. 1(a) Identify and explain two methods PP could use to improve the quality of its products. Knowledge [2 â‹… 1] â€“ Award one mark for each relevant method (maximum of two), Analysis [2 â‹… 1] award one mark for a relevant explanation of the method Application [2 â‹… 2] award two application marks for each method Methods might include: â€¢ Quality control â€“ checked at the end/checked by inspectors/aims to eliminate faults before product gets to customers/less training needed as inspectors employed to check quality â€¢ Quality assurance â€“ checked by employees throughout the production process/eliminates faults before it passes to the next stage of production process/reduced costs as less need to reduce prices of faulty products/more expensive to train staff to check their own work/relies on workers being committed to maintaining a standard Â• Total Quality Management (TQM) â€“ continuous improvement by focusing on each and every stage of the production process/quality become central ethos of employees/â€˜right first timeâ€™ means no customer complaints/reduced costs as no faults to repair or price reductions needed/expensive to train all employees/relies on all employees accepting responsibility for ensuring quality â€¢ (Employees) Additional training of employees/recruit experienced workers â€“ less likely to make mistakes â€¢ (Raw materials) Change supplier/improved quality of raw materials â€“ improved quality of final product if inputs better â€¢ (Equipment) Introduction of automation/new technology â€“ fewer faults as products are standardised Award a maximum of three additional marks for each explanation of how the method ensures quality production â€“ 2 of which must be applied to this context . Indicative response: Quality control [k] is a traditional way the pottery can be checked (ap) at the end of the production process to make sure any faulty products are stopped from being sent out of the factory. [an] This means that a selection of cups and saucers are checked before they are packaged to ensure the products are perfect. (ap) Possible application marks : manufactures pottery items, factory, vases, plates, private limited company, 30 years ago, skilled production workers, traditional methods, job production. There may be other examples in context, which have not been included here. 8 0450/22 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2018 Page 4 of 14 Question Answer Marks

2(a) Identify and explain two methods of primary research PP could use to research the market in country Z. Knowledge [2 â‹… 1] â€“ Award one mark for each relevant method (maximum of two), Analysis [2 â‹… 1] award one mark for a relevant explanation of the method Application [2 â‹… 2] award two application marks for each method Award one mark for each method of primary research PP could use (maximum of two), such as: Â• Questionnaire - lower response rate/relatively cheap Â• Survey â€“ cheap method/easy to collate responses/may not reach target audience Â• Interview â€“ face-to-face completion of questions/qualitative data/interviewer bias Â• Focus group â€“ group of people familiar with the product Â• Consumer panel â€“ randomly selected group of people â€“ opinions gathered/relatively expensive to carry out Â• Observation â€“ trained researchers watch how consumers behave and interact in the market Note: â€“ Survey and Questionnaire = one point unless qualified (e.g. postal/online/face-to-face) Award a maximum of three additional marks for each explanation of the method PP could use â€“ two of which must be applied to this context. Indicative response: Questionnaires (k) could be carried out on the streets to gather consumer opinions. (an). Some of the designs of pottery could be shown to the sample to ask their opinion of the designs. (ap) This will gather the personal opinions of consumers to find out their particular needs for dining sets. (ap) Possible application marks : manufactures pottery items, factory, vases, plates, private limited company, serve different types of food, 30 years ago, skilled production workers, wants to expand by exporting to other countries, traditional methods. 3(a) Identify and explain one advantage and one disadvantage of Peter delegating tasks to the new Operations manager. Knowledge [2 â‹… 1] â€“ Award one mark for each relevant advantage and disadvantage (maximum one for each) Analysis [2 â‹… 1] award one mark for a relevant explanation of the advantage and disadvantage Application [2 â‹… 2] award two application marks for each method Award one mark for each advantage/disadvantage (maximum of two), such as: Advantage â€“ Â• Allows Peter to focus on main tasks as he cannot do every job himself Â• Managers are less likely to make mistakes if some of the tasks are being performed by subordinates Â• Managers can measure the success of their staff more easily Â• The work becomes more interesting for subordinates/subordinates may be more motivated Â• The operations manager feels more important and trusted Â• Allows PP to expand as Peter will need more free time to focus on the expansion Disadvantage â€“ Â• Still responsible if there are errors/some managers are reluctant to delegate/checking and/or monitoring takes time Â• Senior managers lose some control Â• Tasks may not be carried out effectively/makes mistakes/manager might not carry out the tasks effectively so quality may suffer Award a maximum of three additional marks for each explanation of the advantage/disadvantage of delegating tasks to the new operations manager â€“ two of which must be applied to this context . 4(a) Identify and explain two sources of finance PP could use for its expansion. Knowledge [2 â‹… 1] â€“ Award one mark for each relevant source (maximum of two), Analysis [2 â‹… 1] award one mark for a relevant explanation of the source Application [2 â‹… 2] award two marks for application relevant to each source Award one mark for each source of finance (maximum of two), such as: Â• Bank loan â€“ repaid in monthly instalments/interest charged Â• Issue more shares to family and friends/no interest charged/doesnâ€™t need to be repaid/original owners may lose some control Â• Issue shares to public by becoming a public limited company â€“ no interest paid/not repaid/potential for takeover Â• Debentures â€“ fixed interest charged/needs to be repaid after a certain period of time Â• Hire purchase â€“ no initial payment required / not owned until final payment made/paid in instalments/interest charged Â• Leasing â€“ do not own the equipment/repairs not paid for by PP/maybe more expensive in the long run Â• Sale of assets â€“ no repayment needed/may not raise sufficient finance/ takes time to sell assets Â• Retained profit â€“ no need to repay it/no interest to pay/shareholders may not be happy as dividend payment lower Award three additional marks for each explanation of the source of finance â€“ two of which must be applied to this context. Indicative response: Bank loan (k) that will allow a large sum of money e.g. for market research of the new market (ap) to be borrowed and paid back over a long period of time (an) because it will probably take several years to expand and sell in other countries. 1(a) Identify and explain one advantage and one disadvantage to SA of starting to sell new products in a niche market Knowledge [2 â‹… 1] Award one mark for each relevant advantage/disadvantage Analysis [2 â‹… 1] Award one mark for a relevant explanation for each advantage/disadvantage Application [2 â‹… 2] Award two application marks for each advantage/disadvantage Answers are likely to include: Advantage Â• Higher price charged Â• Less competition Â• Meet specific needs of customers Â• Increase sales/market share Â• Spreads risk/diversification Â• Attracts new/different customers Disadvantage Â• More expensive to produce the product â€“ uses batch rather than flow production Â• Increased costs e.g. product development, new marketing strategy needed Â• Limited size of market/limited potential sales Â• Smaller market segment Â• Higher price does not match existing brand image for low priced soap Indicative response: SA can sell specialist products that meets these specific customer needs (k) and SA wants to sell soap aimed at people with older skin. (ap) This means that there are likely to be few competitors in this market as it is smaller and less attractive for larger businesses making it easier to have a higher market share and higher profits. [Turn over 06_0450_21_2019_1.1 *0703178251-I* Soap Additions (SA) SA is a public limited company. It was started 20 years ago in country Z. SA manufactures soap that is sold to the mass market. The soap is produced in a factory employing 100 production workers. The Operations manager thinks that well-motivated employees are important. SA soap is sold for a low price to wholesalers and retailers throughout country Z. The product is sold and packaged either as bars of soap or liquid soap in a bottle. SA has a 50% share of the total market for low price soap. The directors of SA want to expand the business by starting to produce different types of soap aimed at niche markets. The products they want to develop are: â€¢ Luxury soap aimed at high income consumers â€¢ Soaps aimed at improving ageing skin â€¢ Medicated soap to help improve bad skin often experienced by teenagers The manufacturing process for the mass produced soap is automated and uses flow production. However, if the new soaps are produced then they will be manufactured in a new factory using batch production. The new factory and equipment will cost $15m and will require 50 more production workers and a new Operations manager. SA does not use lean production in its existing factory. However, the Managing Director thinks SA would benefit from the introduction of lean production into the existing and the new factory. Some of SAâ€™s managers think they are never involved when important decisions are made. The directors of SA disagree about which leadership style will lead to the best decisions being taken. 06_0450_21_2019_1.1 2 Appendix Newspaper article in Daily News -

This could reduce the market price of their shares in the soap company (ap) as it will take time for the new investment to lead to higher profits and possibly higher dividends (an). Possible application marks: Plc set up 20 years ago; produces soap; sold in a mass market; start selling niche market soaps; need a new factory/equipment; will cost $15m; 50 workers. There may be other examples in context that have not been included here. 8 0450/21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2019 Page 7 of 14 Question Answer Marks

Award one mark for each opportunity/threat (max 4) plus one additional mark for an explanation of each opportunity/threat Opportunities Â• New markets â€“ increased revenue Â• Increased brand awareness Â• Increased sales â€“higher profit Â• Saturated home markets â€“ new markets to develop Â• Spread risks â€“ over more markets/products Â• New products developed â€“ for new markets with different tastes Â• Higher incomes in growing economies â€“ increased disposable income leading to higher sales Â• Free trade agreements â€“ may allow domestic companies easier access to overseas market/Easier to export Â• Access to cheaper supplies/cheaper labour â€“ lowers costs Threats Â• Increased competition in the home market â€“ lose market share â€“ may need to reduce prices Â• Free trade agreements â€“ may allow foreign companies easier access to domestic market Â• Jobs lost in industries that cannot compete with cheaper foreign competition Â• Workers may choose to work for MNC which pays better wages â€“ losing workers Â• Pressure to increase wage rates due to match MNC pay Â• Easier for exploitation by MNCs e.g. labour/natural resources Â• May be more vulnerable to external shocks e.g. recession in main export markets Indicative response: Country Z has signed a trade agreement (1) which provides opportunities for businesses in country Z to sell in new markets abroad and increase revenue (1). 8 0450/21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2019 Page 13 of 14 Question Answer Marks

2(a) Identify and explain two possible reasons for the failure of the business that Peter used to work for. Knowledge [2 â‹… 1] Award one mark for each relevant reason Analysis [2 â‹… 1] Award one mark for an explanation of each reason Application [2 â‹… 2] Award two application marks for each reason Answers are likely to include: Â• Poor management â€“ lack of experience leading to bad decisions Â• Failure to plan for change â€“ not reacting to changes in technology, markets or economic changes Â• Poor financial management â€“ leading to shortage of cash/cash flow problems Â• Over-expansion â€“ problems with finance and management if grows too quickly Indicative response: Poor management (k) as the owner had not been able to pay its suppliers of car parts (ap). The owner did not have the experience to deal with car repair businesses entering in the market (ap) and this may have resulted in cash flow problems as the owner ran out of cash (an). Possible application marks: Motorhome; van conversion; changes vans and large cars; sole trader business; started 2 years ago; financial information from appendix 3; the previous employer lacked management experience; had been unable to pay its suppliers of car parts; car repair business. There may be other examples in context that have not been included here. 8 0450/23 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2019 Page 7 of 14 Question Answer Marks

2(b) Consider the following three ways Peter could recruit a new skilled worker. Recommend which would be the best way to use. Justify your answer. Relevant points might include: Benefits Drawbacks Ask family and friends if they know someone suitable Â• Cheap as no cost involved Â• Quick to carry out Â• Personal qualities known as coming recommended â€“ more likely to be honest Â• May not be a wide range of people known to family/friends Â• Friends may not know the type of person who will be suitable Use a job vacancy website Â• Wide range of people may see it Â• more likely to be seen by someone with suitable skills Â• Suitable people may not see the job advertisement unless they are looking for a new job Â• It may be a website for many different types of jobs and the job may be lost amongst the many jobs advertised on it Use a recruitment agency Â• Saves time as do not need to carry out own recruitment Â• Sends suitable people so saves time interviewing Â• Already gone through earlier selection procedures Â• Have to pay the agency â€“ it is expensive Â• May have to keep paying the agency part of the wages Â• Lower wages for the person recruited â€“ may leave for competitor who pays the going wage rate 12 0450/23 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2019 Page 8 of 14 Question Answer Marks

3(a) Identify and explain two reasons why building customer relationships is important for VC. Knowledge [2 â‹… 1] Award one mark for each relevant reason Analysis [2 â‹… 1] Award one mark for an explanation of each reason Application [2 â‹… 2] Award two application marks for each reason Answers are likely to include: Â• Getting to know customersâ€™ wants by asking them â€“ makes them loyal to the business â€“ retains customers and reputation Â• To identify what customers want from the van conversion â€“ Peter can adapt the service to meet customer needs Â• Peter wants to maintain a good reputation â€“ so customers tell others about the business and increase advertising by word of mouth â€“ increase sales Â• Increase customer loyalty Indicative response: By having a good relationship with customers Peter can ask them about what they want (k). This means Peter must find out what sort of van conversions customers might want. (ap) If Peter then offers these services he will be more likely to attract customers to VC (an) and the business will continue to grow as this is what Peter wants as his main business objective. (ap) Possible application marks: Motorhome; van conversion; changes vans and large cars; sole trader business; started 2 years ago; plans to expand by offering additional services; financial information from Appendix 3; main business objective is growth; job production; every vehicle is different. 4(a) Identify and explain one advantage and one disadvantage to VC of using job production. Knowledge [2 â‹… 1] Award one mark for each relevant advantage/disadvantage Analysis [2 â‹… 1] Award one mark for an explanation of each advantage/disadvantage Application [2 â‹… 2] Award two application marks for each advantage/disadvantage Answers are likely to include: Advantages: Â• It is suitable for these one-off personalised van conversions â€“ each one is different according to customer preferences â€“ can charge higher prices Â• The service meets the exact requirements of the customer â€“ increasing customer satisfaction Â• More interesting and varied work for employees â€“ can be more motivating than flow or batch production Disadvantages: Â• Likely for higher wages to be paid to skilled workers â€“ costs are higher Â• Labour-intensive production often takes longer â€“ as each job is different â€“ it may take more time to complete a customer order Â• Materials used may need to be ordered separately â€“ take time to arrive as made especially to order and any mistakes will be expensive to replace Â• Cannot easily be sold to someone else â€“ materials may have to be specially purchased â€“ increasing costs Indicative response: Service meets the exact requirements of the customer (k) so increases customer satisfaction leading to good image for the business and increase sales. (an) The vans will be converted in the exact way the customer wants (ap) and then when travelling round other people will see the van and if they like the inside, such as a cooker included, then they may be more likely to ask VC to undertake a conversion for them. (ap) Possible application marks: Motorhome; van conversion; changes vans and large cars; sole trader business; started 2 years ago; plans to expand by offering additional services; needs 3 new skilled and experienced workers; financial information from appendix 3; ordering car parts. Answers are likely to include: Â• MF has limited liability Â• There is continuity with MF Â• MF has a separate legal identity Â• MF is able to sell shares Note: Do not accept MF is an incorporated business Indicative response: MF has limited liability whereas an unincorporated business has unlimited liability (1). This protects the shareholders of MF (1). Context is from recognising that MF is an incorporated business â€“ shareholders; MF is a public limited company; MF is not a sole trader or partnership; MF competitors are partnership businesses 4

4(a)(ii) Identify and explain two differences between MF and public sector organisations: Award one mark for each difference (2 max) plus one additional mark for explaining why it is a difference in context. Answers are likely to include: Â• MF is not owned by the government Â• Different objectives such as private sector aim to make profits whereas public sector aim to provide a service to the public/different pricing strategies Â• Investment from the shareholders whereas public sector investment by the government Note: Do not accept MF is an incorporated business Indicative response: MF has profit and increased market share as objectives whereas public sector organisations will have different objectives such as public provision of a service (1) Context is from recognising that MF is a private sector business â€“ MF objective is to increase profit; MF objective is to increase market share; MF is in the private sector; MF may fund investment by selling shares 4 0450/21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2019 Page 13 of 14 Question Answer Marks

4(b) Consider the possible external costs and external benefits resulting from MFâ€™s new product. Recommend whether the Government should encourage MF to produce this new product. Justify your answer. 1(a) Identify and explain two ways QC can create added value for its new service. Knowledge [2 â‹… 1] â€“ award one mark for each way Analysis [2 â‹… 1] â€“ award one mark for a relevant explanation for each way Application [2 â‹… 2] â€“ award two application marks for each way Answers are likely to include: Â• Reward ways of how to set higher prices such as â€“ Improve quality â€“ Provide extra services â€“ Improve brand image Â• Reward ways of how to reduce input costs such as â€“ Buy cheaper ingredients by changing supplier/buying in bulk to gain a discount/negotiating cheaper price â€“ Source cheaper ingredients from home country instead of importing ingredients Indicative response: Set a higher price (1) so that there is a larger difference between the input costs and price. (1) QC could add additional services to the catering such as silver service (ap) so that it provides a USP that makes the catering service better than competitors and therefore clients will be willing to pay a higher price. Justify your answer. Relevant points might include: Â• Questionnaire about the wedding catering market carried out by QC Â• Primary research so original research Â• Gathered for QC specific purpose Â• QC is a small business and therefore could not afford a large sample Â• Not experienced in carrying out primary research so may be less accurate Â• Questionnaire about the wedding catering market carried out by a market research agency Â• Large size sample size Â• Carried out by experts in market research Â• May include information not thought about by Neil and Jin Â• Not specifically about QC target market Â• The whole market might be very different to QC specific target market Â• Expensive for QC to use Â• Government statistics on the number of weddings in country Z Â• Cheap and quick to carry out Â• Easily accessible Â• Gain an idea of overall trends in the market for weddings Â• May be out of date Â• May not be helpful as not specific about wedding catering and may only gain numbers etc. Conclusion might include: Â• Up to date information Â• Expertise used/skill of researchers Â• Sample size Â• Effective questions on questionnaire Â• Specific to QC target market 12 0450/22 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2019 Page 5 of 15 Question Answer Marks

2(a) Neil and the part-time employees are both stakeholders in QC. Identify and explain: Â• one objective that will be the same for both these stakeholders Â• one objective for Neil that will conflict with an objective of the part- time employees. Knowledge [2 â‹… 1] â€“ award one mark for stakeholder objectives that are the same/are conflicting Analysis [2 â‹… 1] â€“ award one mark for a relevant explanation of why the objective is the same/why it is conflicting Application [2 â‹… 2] â€“ award two application marks for explanation of each stakeholder objective in context Answers are likely to include: Â• Owners and employees both want the business to make profits â€“ to pay higher wages and higher dividends/ more likely to survive and employees more likely to keep their jobs. Â• Owner wants to keep costs lower and may reduce wages but employees want higher wages that will put costs up. Likely shareholder/Owner objectives: Â• To increase profit/make a profit Â• To expand/grow the business Â• To increase sales Â• To maintain brand image/high reputation/customer satisfaction Â• To reduce costs Â• To increase productivity/efficiency Â• To survive Likely employee objectives: Â• To receive higher wages Â• To have job security Â• To have improved working conditions Â• To work sociable hours Â• To be motivated and happy at work Indicative response: Owner wants to make a high profit (1) but this could keep down the wages of the employees. (1) There are 90 part-time employees and they may feel unhappy about this. (ap) These employees may be less willing to work additional catering jobs when QC is busy. (ap) Possible application marks: Catering services; two shareholders; wants to expand; Weddings; Birthday parties; events; provides catering for businesses such as for conferences; 90 part-time employees; 10 full-time employees; maintain good reputation. There may be other examples in context that have not been included here. 8 0450/22 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2019 Page 7 of 15 Question Answer Marks

2(b) Consider the advantages and disadvantages of the following three leadership styles Jin and Neil could use. Recommend which one they should use as QC expands further. Justify your answer. Advantages Disadvantages Autocratic Â• Clear direction given to employees Â• Employees know what is expected from them Â• Decisions can be made quickly Â• Communication is only one way Â• Cannot know the views or suggestions of employees Â• May be demotivating as employees are not included in any decision making Democratic Â• May be motivating as employees may be asked before decisions made Â• Feel as though can contribute to the business â€“ feel valued Â• May take a long time to consult employees Â• Decisions may need to be made quickly and this leadership style is too slow Laissez-faire Â• Give decision making to employees Â• Motivating as employees feel they have control over their work Â• Managers have more time for other tasks as can delegate more Â• Employers feel more trusted Â•

4(a) Identify and explain two ways Neil and Jin could improve QCâ€™s cash flow. Knowledge [2 â‹… 1] â€“ award one mark for each way Analysis [2 â‹… 1] â€“ award one mark for a relevant explanation for each way Application [2 â‹… 2] â€“ award two application marks for each way Answers are likely to include: Â• Arrange trade credit for ingredients â€“ delay outflows of cash for payments of ingredients so improves cash flow as money held in account for longer. Â• Reduce trade credit for clients â€“ speeds up inflow of money as payment for catering services are made more quickly so increases speed at which cash flows into account. Â• Arrange an overdraft facility / short term bank loan Â• Change to local supplier as ingredients are cheaper Â• Get deposits from customers before the events Â• Sell off unwanted non-current assets/Sell unused capital asset Â• Increase cash inflows Â• Reduce cash outflows Note: Do not reward advertising unless part of an explanation Note: Bank loan is TV - should be short-term bank loan Indicative response: Neil can arrange trade credit (1) for ingredients purchased from abroad. (ap) This will delay outflow of cash for payments of ingredients so improves cash flow as money held in account for longer. (1) So QC will keep money in its account for longer and make it easier to pay the wages of the 90 part-time employees. (ap) Possible application marks: Catering services; wants to expand; weddings; birthday parties; events; provides catering for businesses such as for conferences; 90 part-time employees;10 full-time employees; imports food ingredients; offers trade credit; pays cash for ingredients; plans to expand; two shareholders; may run out of working capital. Knowledge [2 â‹… 1] â€“ award one mark for each relevant reason Analysis [2 â‹… 1] â€“ award one mark for a relevant explanation of each reason Application [2 â‹… 2] â€“ award two application marks for each reason Answers are likely to include: â€¢ To possibly increase profit/Increase revenue â€¢ Higher status and prestige for owner â€¢ Lower average costs/Benefit from economies of scale â€¢ Larger share of the market/Increase market share â€¢ Spread risk Indicative response: To possibly increase profit (1) because if the restaurant is bigger it will have more customers and more revenue (ap). 1(b) Consider the advantages and disadvantages of the following three types of business organisation. Recommend which type of business organisation Richard should choose for RR as it expands. Justify your answer. Sole trader Â• Owner in complete control Â• No sharing of profit Â• Incentive to work hard as everything kept for himself Â• Unlimited liability Â• No continuity Â• No one to help share decision making Partnership Â• Partners can specialise in different tasks Â• Share decision making Â• More ideas/experience Â• Able to raise more capital from partners Â• Unlimited liability Â• Each partner responsible for the actions of other partners Â• No continuity Private limited company Â• Raise capital from sale of shares Â• Limited liability Â• Separate legal identity Â• Continuity Â• Can only sell shares to family and friends Â• Accounts not private Â• Legal formalities when setting up and running the company. 12 0450/23 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2019 Page 5 of 14 Question Answer Marks

Award 1 mark for each relevant advantage/disadvantage (maximum of two): Relevant advantages might include: â€¢ May have no initial fees to be paid to the crowd-funding platform â€¢ Allows public reaction to the new business venture to be tested â€¢ Can be a fast way to raise substantial sums of money â€¢ May be used when other more â€˜traditionalâ€™ sources are not available Relevant disadvantages might include: â€¢ Crowd-funding platforms may reject Simonâ€™s proposal if it is not well thought through â€¢ If total amount required is not raised, then the finance promised will have to be repaid â€¢ Interest from the public will need to be generated to increase the chance of successfully raising the amount required â€¢ May allow competitors to steal the idea and reach the market first Award a maximum of 3 additional marks for each explanation of the advantage and disadvantage of using crowd-funding. â€“ one of which must be applied to this context. For example: Crowd-funding will allow large amounts of capital to be raised (1) so Simon can start producing a large output of fruit drinks quickly (app). This will allow Simon to enter the new market before competitors can copy his drinks (1) and he will not have to pay back the money, unlike a bank loan. (1) 8 0450/21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2020 Page 7 of 14 Question Answer Marks
