1(a) Explain one benefit and one problem for ES of taking over other businesses. Award 1 mark for each relevant benefit/problem (maximum of two): Relevant benefits might include: â€¢ Assets already set up for use â€“ can use existing scooter factories and skilled employees â€¢ Makes it quicker to grow â€“ as the existing production facilities can be used to increase the output of scooters â€“ faster than setting up own factories â€¢ Have access to ready trained/skilled staff Relevant problems might include: â€¢ High cost of buying other business â€“ may need to sell additional shares on the stock exchange â€¢ May be difficult integrating the two companies â€“ as the way of working may be very different in the smaller scooter businesses â€“ and employees may not like the changes â€“ leading to less efficiency â€¢ More difficult to control larger business â€“ many more skilled employees in the business â€“ possible diseconomies of scale Award a maximum of 3 additional marks for each explanation of the benefits and problems of growing by taking over other businesses â€“ one of which must be applied to this context . 2(a) Explain one advantage and one disadvantage for ECâ€™s senior managers when they delegate tasks. Award 1 mark for each relevant advantage/disadvantage (maximum of two): Relevant advantages might include: â€¢ able to concentrate their time on other important management functions â€“ as they complain that they have too many tasks to do â€“ delegating tasks will allow them to concentrate on the most important tasks â€“ leading to better decisions being made â€¢ less likely to make mistakes if some of the tasks are carried out by others â€“ have time to focus on specific tasks â€“ not rushed or too many other tasks to complete taking up time â€¢ can measure tasks performed by juniors more easily â€“ easier to measure the success of their staff â€“ more motivating Relevant disadvantages might include: â€¢ may be afraid subordinates perform the tasks better than them â€“ makes them insecure â€¢ subordinates may make mistakes â€“ as they may not be trained to perform all the tasks they have been asked to do â€“ for example order the wrong components for the cars â€¢ tasks are still the senior managers responsibility if things go wrong â€“ if junior managers carrying out the tasks make mistakes â€“ it is the senior managers who will have to sort out the problems Award a maximum of 3 additional marks for each explanation of the advantage/disadvantage of delegation for senior managersâ€“ one of which must be applied to this context . For example: The senior managers will be able to concentrate their time on other important management functions (1) as they often complain they have too many tasks to do (app). If the senior managers are carrying out too many basic tasks it will allow less time to focus on more important decisions such as developing a new car (1) making it more likely for mistakes to be made or opportunities missed (1). 8 0450/23 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2020 Page 7 of 14 Question Answer Marks

3(a) Explain why liquidity and profitability are important to a business. Award 1 mark for each relevant reason or explanation of the reason for liquidity and profitability: Relevant reasons might include: Liquidity: â€¢ Allows a business to see if it is able to pay back the short-term debts of the business â€¢ Measured by current ratio/acid test ratio â€¢ If it cannot pay the debts, then it will not be able to buy more inventory or raw materials â€¢ If the business becomes illiquid it may be forced to sell business assets to pay debts and will cease trading Profitability: â€¢ It measures how well profit is being made from sales or capital invested in the business â€¢ Could be used to reinvest into the business/use retained profits for expansion â€¢ Measured by gross profit margin/profit margin/ROCE â€¢ It can be a measure of efficiency â€¢ Can compare business performance over time/compare against other businesses â€¢ It helps investors or bank to decide whether to invest in the business or lend it money â€¢ Retain shareholders/pay dividends/return on investment for the owners Award a maximum of 3 additional marks for each explanation. This is a generic question, therefore there are no application marks available. For example: Profitability is a measure of performance (1) and can be used to show the bank so that it may give the business a loan for expansion. (1) It shows how efficiently the business is converting investment into profit (1) as shown by the percentage of profit to revenue or profit to investment in the business. (1) 8 0450/21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2020 Page 10 of 16 Question Answer Marks

Relevant reasons might include: â€¢ Change in the primary sector â€“ increase due to new discovery of natural resources / decreases due to depletion of natural resources â€¢ Change in the secondary sector â€“ increase due to industrialisation / decreases due to deindustrialisation â€¢ Change in the tertiary sector â€“ increase due to increased consumer incomes â€¢ Increase in incomes / economic growth / increase in GDP â€“ this could lead to a change in the secondary or tertiary sector â€¢ Increase in demand for manufactured goods â€“ increase in the size of secondary sector â€¢ Increase in tertiary sector â€“ as consumers have higher disposable income to spend on services â€¢ Increased mechanisation / increased technology â€“ reduces number of employees in the secondary sector â€¢ People move into the tertiary sector to provide services â€“ as demand for services grows as a country develops e.g. tourism â€¢ Increase in exported products â€“ more need to be manufactured in secondary sector â€¢ Reduced employment in primary sector â€“ as now primary products produced using mechanisation / rural to urban migration â€¢ Primary products are now processed in own country â€“ increased manufacturers / secondary sector to process the raw materials into finished products â€¢ Government policies to encourage development â€¢ Increased globalisation â€¢ Actions of environmental groups / pressure groups leading to sectoral change â€“ primary extraction or dirty manufacturing in the secondary sector For example: Incomes have increased (1) and consumers have more purchasing power for manufactured goods increasing the importance of the secondary sector (1). 8 0450/21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 6 of 17 Question Answer Marks

Relevant advantages might include: â€¢ Have a wider range of pushchairs in the shop / better quality â€“ meet the needs of more customers â€“ sell more pushchairs â€“ higher revenue â€¢ Fill a gap in the market for foreign branded products â€“ satisfy those customer needs â€“ higher-income customers may prefer these brands â€¢ May be purchased at lower cost price â€“ lower selling price in the shop â€“ increase demand/sales â€¢ These pushchairs may not be manufactured locally Relevant disadvantages might include: â€¢ May have to pay higher prices if tariffs are imposed â€“ raises the cost of inventory for TT â€“ may lead to higher prices in the shop â€¢ May have quotas that limit the quantity imported â€“ restricts the supply/availability of products in the shop â€“ may lead to higher prices as a result of the shortages â€¢ Increased costs of finding foreign suppliers â€“ have to contact many suppliers in other countries to order inventory â€“ takes Tandeep time to do this when he could be doing other tasks â€¢ Increased delivery time â€“ as the inventory has further to travel than if produced in country Z â€“ may delay goods ordered by customers â€¢ Imported pushchairs may not meet the same standards as locally produced pushchairs â€“ may lead to pushchairs breaking â€“ leading to a poor reputation for TT â€“ lose customers to competitors â€¢ Possibly more damaged goods â€“ higher costs as have to replace damaged goods â€“ reduces profit â€¢ Risk of exchange rate fluctuations affecting cost price of inventories â€“ lower exchange rate will lead to higher import costs For example: This may mean Tandeep will have a wider range of pushchairs in the shop (1) and this may appeal to high income customers who are their target market (app) meaning Tandeep will have higher sales (1). Relevant reasons might include: â€¢ To see if there is a cash flow problem / liquidity problem â€“ if so, he needs to organise an overdraft â€“ the cash-flow forecast will help support Tandeep persuading the bank to give an overdraft / bank loan. (Other ways of solving a cash flow problem might be given, e.g. apply for trade credit) â€¢ To see if there is sufficient cash / working capital to pay day-to-day costs â€“ so, TT can pay expenses â€“ without the need for additional working capital â€¢ Whether the company is holding too much cash â€“ perhaps there may be better uses of the cash â€“ that may yield a greater return on the capital â€¢ May help with decisions/planning about when to purchase assets/equipment/inventory â€¢ Help to sell shares to new investors â€¢ Can be used to compare financial performance, against actual performance over time (e.g. sales) â€¢ Can be used to help apply for a bank loan For example: Tandeep may use the cash-flow forecast to take to the bank to support the application for an overdraft (1) as in the first month of trading he will need to borrow $200 to keep trading (app). 1(b) Define â€˜working capitalâ€™. Award 2 marks for a full definition. Award 1 mark for a partial definition. 1(a) Explain two benefits and two drawbacks of specialisation within a manufacturing process. Award 1 mark for each benefit/drawback (maximum of 4). Award a maximum of 1 additional mark for each explanation of why it is a benefit/drawback of specialisation within a manufacturing process. Relevant benefits might include: â€¢ Higher quantity produced / higher output â€“ increased sales â€¢ Production workers are trained in one task â€“ quicker to train / cheaper to train workers â€¢ Quicker to produce output / increased efficiency â€“ as less time taken by workers moving between tasks in the factory â€¢ Performing only one task makes worker become a skilled expert / employee specialises in what they are good at â€“ more efficient in that task / more job satisfaction â€¢ Easier to use machinery to perform tasks â€“ speeds up production â€¢ Fewer mistakes made â€“ less wastage / fewer customer complaints / reduced costs of production Relevant drawbacks might include: â€¢ Production workers may become bored doing the same task repeatedly â€“ may become less efficient and slow down production â€¢ Workers less motivated / less job satisfaction â€“ more likely to leave the business â€¢ Workers cannot cover for absent colleagues â€“ manufacturing may halt or slow down â€¢ Worker may find it difficult to find alternative employment â€“ as only trained/skilled in one task â€¢ Lose other skills â€“ as only use skills for one task For example: The production workers are trained in only one task (1) therefore it takes less time to train new recruits to the factory. (1) 8 0450/21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2021 Page 6 of 17 Question Answer Marks

Limited ability to discuss the problems with little/no explanation. Simple judgement with limited justification / limited evaluation of choices made. Candidates outlining all three problems in context should be rewarded with the top marks in the band. 1â€“4

Relevant answers might include: Methods of measuring size Relevant points might include: Number of people employed â€¢ Easy to calculate and compare with other airports â€¢ Workers may be full-time or part-time and this may distort the comparison Capital employed â€¢ Easy to calculate the value of the assets in the business â€¢ However, may be similar to number of employees, it may be capital or labour intensive and therefore difficult to compare the size of the airports Value of sales â€¢ Often used when comparing the sales of different retail outlets â€¢ Can be misleading when comparing businesses that provide/sell very different services/products Value of output â€¢ Easy way to compare manufacturing industries but more difficult with service sector businesses â€¢ Can be measured by the number of flights but this may not be accurate as there may be many small aeroplanes carrying few passengers and other airports may have a few large aeroplanes carrying many passengers â€¢ However, may have many different types of services â€“ difficult to compare an airport with shipments of goods and others with only passengers â€¢ Value of output may be high, but the number of flights could be low and therefore not an accurate measure 8 0450/23 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2021 Page 9 of 18 Question Answer Marks

4(a) Explain four factors that might affect the source of finance a business decides to use. Award 1 mark for each relevant factor (maximum of four factors). Award a maximum of 1 additional mark for each explanation of the factor that will affect the choice of the source of finance for a business. Relevant factors might include: Relevant factors Ways the factor will affect the choice of source of finance Size and legal form of business â€¢ An incorporated business can issue new shares whereas an unincorporated business cannot â€¢ Large businesses can access sources of finance that small businesses cannot such as some specialist banks Amount required â€¢ A large amount may only be available as a bank loan rather than as an overdraft Length of time â€¢ If the finance required is for the short-term then only short-term sources should be considered such as trade credit or overdraft Existing loans â€¢ If the business already has loans, then it may be difficult to take out further loans â€“ may need to find alternative sources of finance such as selling assets Purpose for using the finance â€¢ If the purpose is for expansion so long-term sources should be used as the business has a longer time to earn the revenue to repay the amount borrowed Control â€¢ The original owners may not want to lose control of the business. If shares are sold to other people or partners are added to a partnership, then more people will be investing in the business and the original owners may lose control. For example: The purpose for using the finance. (1) If the finance is required for expansion then a long-term source, such as a bank loan should be chosen (1). 8 0450/23 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2021 Page 17 of 18 Question Answer Marks

1(a) Define â€˜competitive pricingâ€™. Award 2 marks for a full definition. Award 1 mark for a partial definition. When a product is priced in line or just below competitorsâ€™ prices (to try to capture more of the market) [2] OR Setting a price similar to that of competitors products which are already established in the market. 2(a) Explain one benefit and one limitation to Yasin of using a break-even chart. Award 1 mark for each benefit/limitation (maximum of two marks). Award a maximum of 3 additional marks for each explanation of the benefit/limitation of using the break-even chartâ€“ one of which must be applied to this context. Relevant benefits might include: ï‚· Shows break-even output â€“ so Yasin knows how much bread he needs to sell to break even â€“ this will allow him to plan the bakery and equipment required â€“ to ensure at least the minimum quantity of bread is produced and sold ï‚· Shows the margin of safety â€“ this is the amount of bread Yasin predicts will be sold above the break-even output â€“ it provides a target for sales of bread â€“ so that MB makes a profit ï‚· Helps in decision making â€“ so that Yasin knows the minimum target amount of bread to produce â€“ he can see if he needs to increase the price to break-even more quickly â€“ or if lower ingredient costs is required by MB to make a profit ï‚· Shows the area of profit or loss â€“ and therefore what the output needs to be to cover all costs â€“ if the market research shown in the business plan does not indicate that sufficient sales will be made to make a profit â€“ then Yasin may need to think again about his plans for the bakery. Relevant limitations might include: ï‚· A break-even chart assumes all the output will be sold/provides an estimate of sales â€“ but MB may not sell all the bread in the day â€“ some may be wasted and the costs of producing these will not be taken into account â€“ so at a specific output the business may not make a profit but may make a loss ï‚· The break-even chart is drawn on the assumption of straight lines â€“ but when the output is higher Yasin may be able to bulk buy ingredients â€“ this may mean he can receive discounts so the variable cost per unit may not be constant ï‚· Fixed costs/variable costs may change â€“ if the scale of production changes as the bakery expands â€“ the rent or depreciation of equipment will change/ingredient costs may change ï‚· Need a separate break-even chart for each product/need a new chart if prices are changed â€“ relevant if going to make cakes For example: The chart shows the break-even output for MB (1) so Yasin will know how many units of bread he will need to sell each month to break even (app). This will allow him to plan for the equipment required (1) so he can ensure the bakery sells the minimum amount of bread to cover all the costs and not make a loss (1). Application could include: bread; bakery; cakes; data presented in Appendix 2; fixed costs will change if either of the options in Appendix 2 go ahead. 8 0450/21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2022 Page 9 of 18 Question Answer Marks

This means cash has left the business to pay for raw materials but there is a delay in receiving cash in from business customers ï‚· No trade credit â€“ cash leaves the business immediately when food ingredients are delivered from the suppliers ï‚· If the costs of expansion are higher than forecast this will lead to higher cash outflows/repayment of the interest on the loan â€“ expansion may take a long time to increase cash inflows ï‚· Poor management decisions as store managers are overworked â€“ they donâ€™t have time to look for the best deal to try and reduce the cash outflows / mistakes are made when preparing an order which means that sandwiches have to be made again For example: BB has high levels of credit sales (1) the company currently allows business customers to pay at the end of the month (app). The business has to buy all the ingredients for the sandwiches in advance but does not receive any income for up to one month later (1). This means that there is a delay between cash paid out to suppliers and cash being received from the customers (1). 3(a) Explain one advantage and one disadvantage to Tokumbo of being a sole trader. 1 mark for each advantage/disadvantage identified (max 2). Award a maximum of 3 additional marks for explanation of each advantage/disadvantage for Tokumbo of being a sole trader â€“ one of which must be app lied to this context. Relevant advantages might include: â€¢ Owner is in complete control / makes all the decisions â€“ likes being his own boss with no one to tell him what to do â€“ prefers making all the business decisions instead of having to consult others â€“ such as deciding whether to start offering other safari tours â€¢ Does not have to share profits â€“ as there is only one owner â€“ keeps all the returns from his own hard work â€“ his 5 employees â€¢ It was easy to set - up â€“ as there were few legal requirements â€“ reduced the costs of setting up, such as accountancy costs â€¢ Freedom to choose own working hours or holidays â€“ if worked for another business he would have to work the hours stated in his employment contract â€“ which he would have done when he worked for a holiday company â€“ no flexibility to change these hours if needed â€¢ Complete privacy â€“ does not have to share any financial details with anyone else â€“ apart from the tax office, as he will still have to pay tax Relevant disadvantages might include: â€¢ Unlimited liability / no limited liability â€“ which means all his personal possessions are at risk â€“ not just the capital he invested in the business â€¢ There is no - one to share responsibilities with or discuss business decisions â€“ everything is the s ole responsibility of the owner â€“ this can be stressful, and he may be more likely to make mistakes â€¢ No continuity â€“ if the owner dies then because the owner is the business it will end â€“ unlike a company where the shares allow it to carry on as it has a separate legal identity â€¢ Lack of capital / difficult to raise finance â€“ as only the funds of the sole trader or borrowing is available to the business â€“ may make it more difficult to expand in the future â€¢ May need to work long hours / difficult to take holid ays / increased workload For example: A disadvantage is that Tokumbo has unlimited liability (1) and he put his own money into the business to start it 2 years ago (app). If SA fails, he could lose all his personal possessions (1) and not just the capital he invested in SA as would happen if it was a limited company (1). Application could include: safari holidays; increase in tourists from other countries; high income tourists; luxury safari holidays; 5 employees; only been operating for 2 years; game res erve; camping / tent; wild animals; 4 safari guides; 4 safari vehicles; accommodation in local / luxury hotels; fuel. 8 0450 / 21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 20 22 22 Page 15 of 22 Question Answer Marks

4(a) Explain two possible effects on SA of the increase in the rate of inflation in country X. Award 1 mark for each effect (max 2). Award a maximum of 3 additional marks for each explanation of an effect on SA of an increase in the rate of inflation in country X â€“ one of which must be applied to this context. Relevant answers might include: â€¢ Likely rise in prices for safari tours â€“ decreased demand / harder to attract tourists to country X â€“ decreased revenue for SA if demand is price elastic â€“ possible decrease in profits if costs rise more than revenue â€¢ Increase in costs for SA â€“ may need to spend time sourcing lower cost inputs / updating the brochures â€“ keep recalculating prices / need to keep printing brochures with new prices â€¢ More pressure to increase wages for SA employees â€“ prices of basic goods may be increasing significantly in country X â€“ as employees cannot maintain their standard of living without a wage increase â€“ increases costs â€¢ Increased cost of investment / equipment â€“ may mean expansion is delayed Fo r example: Prices of basic goods may be increasing significantly in country X (1) as the inflation rate is now 4% (app). This will mean there is more pressure on SA to increase the wages of employees (1) because employees cannot maintain their standard of living without a wage increase (1). Application could include: safari holidays; increase in tourists from other countries; high income tourists; luxury safari holidays; 5 employees; only been operating for 2 years; sole trader; game reserve; camping / tent; wild animals; 4 safari guides; 4 safari vehicles; accommodation in local / luxury hotels; fuel. 8 0450 / 21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 20 22 22 Page 20 of 22 Question Answer Marks

1(a) Explain two reasons why the owners of CC might want to expand the business. Award 1 mark for each relevant reason (max 2). Award a maximum of 3 additional marks for each explanation of why the owners of CC might want to expand the business â€“ one of which must be applied to this context. Relevant answers might include: â€¢ To possibly gain higher profits for the owners â€“ as expansion will lead to increased sales in new markets â€“ leading to increased revenue â€“ CC shareholders may want higher dividends paid / increase in share price â€¢ To lower average/unit costs â€“ gain from economies of scale â€“ such as bulk buying of cocoa beans â€“ a discount could be received f or bulk purchases â€¢ To gain a larger share of the market â€“ increase in the proportion of total sales in the market may increase revenue â€“ as the home market is saturated so limited potential for growth in this home market â€¢ To gain more status and prestige for the owners â€“ may be listed on stock exchanges in other countries â€¢ To spread risk / diversify markets For example: To possibly gain higher profits for the owners (1) because expansion is likely to increase revenue, (1) as CC will sell its products in more markets (1). The higher profits should allow the shareholders of CC to be paid higher dividends (app). Diseconomies might include: â€¢ Poor communication â€“ more layers of hierarchy for messages to be transmitted after the takeo ver of NM â€“ more likely to be distorted â€¢ Lack of commitment from employees â€“ larger businesses may make employees feel unimportant as there are so many employees in the organisation / lower or poor morale â€“ increased absenteeism or higher labour turnover â€“ increased average costs â€¢ Weak coordination â€“ decisions may be taken by some managers in country X that do not fit well with other department decisions elsewhere in the business â€“ leading to a lack of efficiency â€¢ Lack of control â€“ because a larger business ma y not have a close working relationship with all employees â€“ so some employees may not be motivated to work hard producing chocolates â€“ has higher unit costs from employing and training more supervisors For example: There may be poor morale amongst the em ployees (1) because a larger business may make employees feel unimportant as there are so many employees in the organisation (1). Award a maximum of three additional marks for each explanation of the reason why VP wants to expand, one of which must be in context . Answers might include: ï‚· To gain higher profits for the owners â€“ as expansion will lead to increased sales in new markets abroad â€“ leading to increased revenue ï‚· To lower average/unit costs â€“ gain from economies of scale â€“ such as bulk buying of food ingredients â€“ a discount will be received from bulk purchases ï‚· To gain a larger share of the market â€“ may produce a stronger brand image â€“ might result in greater customer loyalty ï‚· To gain more status and prestige for the owners â€“ from the company being more well known in country Z â€“ may be listed on stock exchanges in other countries ï‚· To gain access to new markets/diversification â€“ this would reduce/spread risk For example: To gain higher profits for the owners (1) because expansion is likely to increase revenue, (1) as VP will sell its products in more markets (1). The higher profits should allow the shareholders of VP to be paid higher dividends (app). Application could include: fast food; restaurant; vegetarians; meals; food outlets across the country; diners; shareholders/public limited company; ingredients. 8 0450/21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2023 Page 15 of 21 Question Answer Marks

4(a) Explain two leadership styles that Zane and Zaara could use when managing ETâ€™s employees. One mark for each leadership style (max 2). Award a maximum of 3 additional marks for each leadership style â€“ one of which must be applied to this context. Relevant answers might include: Autocratic ï‚· The manager expects to be in charge and have all orders followed ï‚· Manager keeps themselves separate from the employees ï‚· Manager makes all the decisions and keeps information to themselves ï‚· Communication is one-way/employees having no opportunity to comment or take part in any decisions about business ï‚· Decision-making is quick Democratic ï‚· Gets employees involved in decision-making about the business ï‚· Information about future plans for the business shared with employees and discussed before owners make the final decision ï‚· Communication is both downward and upward allowing the experience of employees to be considered by the owners â€“ leading to possibly better decisions being made ï‚· Motivating for employees as they feel more involved in the business â€“ less likely to leave/reduced labour turnover Laissez-faire ï‚· Main objectives of the new business are shared with employees but then they are left to organise their own work and take decisions without any interference ï‚· Communication can be difficult as the leader has little involvement in the decisions being made ï‚· Employees can be motivated as they are valued and trusted to take the decisions ï‚· Employees can be creative with fresh ideas about how to operate the business For example: They could use a democratic leadership style (1) where communication is both downward and upward allowing the experience of employees to be considered by the owners (1) leading to possibly better decisions being made (1) about how to operate the theatre (app). 4(a) Explain one opportunity and one threat to RR of using ecommerce. Award one mark for a relevant opportunity/threat (max 2). Award a maximum of three additional marks for each explanation â€“ one of which must be applied to this context . Opportunities Threats ï‚· Increase in demand for the product â€“ due to an increase in convenience for the customers ï‚· If the website is attractive and easy to use customers may purchase more products than they originally intended ï‚· Prices can be changed easily â€“ reflecting the level of demand â€“ leading to higher revenue ï‚· Website can be used to promote products worldwide â€“ may be cheaper than other forms of marketing ï‚· Sales volume may fall â€“ as customers can more easily compare competitor products/prices so may switch to an alternative producer ï‚· It can be expensive to hire a website designer â€“ good website design is needed to ensure it attracts consumers and is easy to navigate ï‚· Transport costs may be high â€“ individual orders will be shipped to separate addresses and not to a central shop/location for distribution ï‚· No face-to-face contact with customers â€“ this is potentially a lost opportunity for market research ï‚· Inventory costs could be higher as more stocks may need to be kept in a warehouse to ensure customer orders can be met in the new markets For example: Customers can more easily compare competitor prices (1) so may switch to an alternative manufacturer of rugs (app) leading to sales volumes falling (1) which may result in a lower market share (1). Application could include: private limited business; rugs; wool; new markets in other countries / expansion into new markets; new technology required; 30 employees; standard patterns; ecommerce; developing new website; operating for 5 years; excellent customer service. 8 0450/23 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2023 Page 18 of 19 Question Answer Marks

2(a) Explain two advantages and two disadvantages of using external recruitment to find EPâ€™s new manager. Award 1 mark for each relevant advantage/disadvantage (max 2 advantages and max 2 disadvantages). Award 1 additional mark for each explanation of the advantage or disadvantage in context. Relevant advantages might include: ï‚· There will be new ideas in the business â€“ which may make the business more competitive with other pottery manufacturers ï‚· There may be no rivalry between the new manager and existing employees â€“ there will not be jealousy amongst the 7 marketing employees who did not get the promotion ï‚· The quality of the candidates may be high/more experienced/more skilled ï‚· Wider choice of applicants to choose from ï‚· There will be no new vacancy to replace the internal applicant Relevant disadvantages might include: ï‚· Recruitment may take longer â€“ as advertisements are needed which may take time to be read and respond to applicants/interviewing applicants for the job of Marketing Manager ï‚· The person is not known to the business â€“ risk of employing someone who does not have the skills and expertise required to understand the production of products such as cups and bowls ï‚· May be more expensive than internal recruitment ï‚· May be less effective for a time after first being employed â€“ as the new person will take time to understand the tall organisational structure of EP ï‚· It may be demotivating for existing employees as not able to be promoted For example: There may be a wider choice of applicants (1) who have more experience in the production of pottery products (app). 3(a) Explain four reasons why consumer spending patterns for a product might change. Award 1 mark for each reason why consumer spending patterns may have changed (max 4). Award a maximum of one additional mark for each explanation of the reason. There are no application marks for this question. Relevant reasons might include: ï‚· Changes in consumer tastes and fashions â€“ increased/decreased demand for the products ï‚· Products no longer meet customer needs â€“ choose to buy alternative products instead ï‚· Changes in incomes â€“ now/no longer able to afford better quality products ï‚· The price of a product changes â€“ higher prices usually lead to lower sales ï‚· Changes in the prices of competitorsâ€™ products â€“ if competitorsâ€™ products are cheaper then customers will buy from them instead ï‚· Spending on advertising and promotional activities â€“ if effective then is likely to result in sales increasing for the successfully advertised product ï‚· Changes in the population/age structure of the population â€“ an increase in the number of older people in the population will increase demand for products that are popular with them ï‚· New technology means new products become available â€“ this will increase demand for these products/demand for old technology products decreases For example â€“ There are changes in consumer tastes and fashions (1) so if a product becomes less fashionable then demand for these products will decrease (1). 8 0450/21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 14 of 21 Question Answer Marks

4(a) Explain two factors EP should consider when choosing a source of finance. Award 1 mark for each factor (max 2). Award a maximum of 3 additional marks for each explanation of a factor that affects which source of finance to choose â€“ one of which must be applied to this context . Relevant answers might include: ï‚· Legal form of business â€“ EP is a private limited company so share issue is an option â€“ and new shares can be sold to raise finance ï‚· Amount required â€“ if the finance is required to improve cash flow then it may not be a very large amount â€“ and it is likely sources such as an overdraft will provide sufficient funds ï‚· Length of time to repay â€“ if the finance is required for a short length of time then trade credit might be suitable â€“ as this is repaid to the supplier within 30/60 days â€“ if needed for long period of time then other sources may be more suitable ï‚· Existing loans â€“ if the business already has several loans, then this may not be an option â€“ due to the already high expense of interest payments on the loans ï‚· Control of the business â€“ the original owners may not want to lose control and therefore may not choose to sell more shares â€“ so the control and ownership of the business remains with the original owners ï‚· Purpose for using the finance ï‚· Size of business ï‚· The interest cost involved with some sources of finance â€“ which will increase costs and lower profit margins For example: The purpose for using the finance. (1) If the finance is required for ways to improve efficiency at EP (app) then a source such as a bank loan could be chosen (1) as this finance can be used to increase efficiency and then repaid over a longer period of time as unit costs are reduced and profitability increases (1). Application could include: pottery; cups; plates; bowls; trading for 40 years; private limited company; high-quality products; want sales to increase; plastic or bamboo products; wants to increase efficiency; improving labour skills; more automation and technology; information from Appendix 2. 8 0450/21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 19 of 21 Question Answer Marks

1(a) Explain two reasons why setting business objectives is important to JJ. Award 1 mark for each reason (max 2). Award a maximum of 3 additional marks for each explanation of the reason why setting business objectives is important â€“ one of which must be applied to this context. Relevant reasons might include: ï‚· A clear target/aim/purpose/goal to work towards â€“ guides the business in the right direction â€“ avoids loss of focus ï‚· Decision-making will be focused on the objectives â€“ meaning better decisions are taken â€“ the business may be more efficient ï‚· Helps motivate employees â€“ objectives will help focus the employees and management to increase efficiency â€“ making it more likely to be achieved ï‚· Comparison of performance â€“ so business managers can compare how each department has performed against their objectives - to see whether or not it has been successful ï‚· Allows measurement of success â€“ can judge performance or progress ï‚· Helps the business to produce a plan or strategy for the business to achieve these objectives For example: To give a clear target for employees and managers to work towards (1) so they all know what is expected of them (1) when producing different items of jewellery (app). Therefore, this means the target is more likely to be achieved (1). Application could include: jewellery; set up 25 years ago; public limited company; objective to increase profit every year; to expand the business; sold directly to customers in other countries; 30% sold using ecommerce; 70% sold in jewellery shops; raw materials purchased locally; 60 full-time employees; 25 part-time employees; bracelets; rings; earrings. 8 0450/22 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 7 of 18 Question Answer Marks

2(a) Explain two benefits and two limitations for JJ of employing part-time workers in its factory. Award 1 mark for each relevant benefit/limitation (max 4). Award a maximum of 1 additional mark for each explanation of the benefit/limitation in context. Relevant benefits might include: ï‚· The employees are more flexible in the hours they work â€“ than the 60 full- time workers ï‚· Ask/more likely for the part-time workers to increase their hours â€“ during busy times to meet demand for bracelets ï‚· Can/more likely increase operating hours in the factory when demand increases â€“ as the 25 part-time workers may be willing to work extra hours ï‚· Part-time employees often do not need to take time off for appointments, such as to visit the doctor â€“ less likely to cause disruption to the manufacture of rings ï‚· May be able to attract skilled employees who want to work reduced hours ï‚· May reduce business costs if pay lower wages than wage rates for full- time workers ï‚· It may be easier to make part-time workers redundant Relevant limitations might include: ï‚· If the employee sees the job as temporary, then they may be less committed to the business - produce less jewellery ï‚· Takes longer to recruit two part-time employees than one full-time employee â€“ so less time to focus on selling in other countries ï‚· There may be an increase in induction and training costs â€“ which may make it difficult to increase profit each year ï‚· More difficult to communicate with part-time employees ï‚· If the job is temporary then less likely to provide training for them For example: If the employee sees the job as temporary, then they may be less committed to the business (1) and produce fewer items of jewellery (app). Application could include: jewellery; set up 25 years ago; public limited company; objective to increase profit every year; to expand the business; sold directly to customers in other countries; 30% sold using ecommerce; 70% sold in jewellery shops; raw materials purchased locally; 60 full-time employees; 25 part-time employees; bracelets; rings; earrings. 8 0450/22 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 10 of 18 Question Answer Marks

Benefits might include: ï‚· Provides a target output RF needs to sell before a profit is made â€“ at the break-even output of 10 items ï‚· The impact of business decisions can be shown by redrawing the chart â€“ if the variable costs increase from $4 per month ï‚· It can be used to show a margin of safety to protect against losses if a fall in sales occurs â€“ from the current margin of safety of 10 Limitations might include: ï‚· The break-even chart is drawn assuming all goods produced are sold - forecast sales are 20 items of furniture per month ï‚· Fixed costs will only remain fixed if the scale of production does not change â€“ at $50 per month ï‚· Revenue may not be the same â€“ RF may decide to offer a 10% discount ï‚· Can only be used for a single product â€“ RF sells a range of furniture and not a single item priced the at the same price ï‚· It assumes that variable/total costs can be drawn as straight lines For example: It will provide a target of how much RF needs to sell each month before they make a profit (1). 2(a) Explain two functions of management for CCâ€™s new manager . Award one mark for each named function (max 2). Award a maximum of 3 additional marks for each explanation of the function of management â€“ one of which must be applied to this context. Functions might include: â€¢ Planning â€“ setting aims and targets for the business â€“ will give a sense of direction with common targets to work towards â€¢ Organising â€“ arranges the tasks for the employees â€“ as a manager cannot do all tasks and will need to delegate â€¢ Coordinating â€“ bringing together the different departments in the organisation â€“ so tasks are carried out efficiently â€¢ Commanding â€“ instructs the employees on how to carry out their tasks â€“ gives guidance as required â€¢ Controlling - checking the employees work so that the expected output is produced each week â€“ and that all aims are achieved For example: Planning (1) setting aims and targets for the business (1) such as sales targets for the cookies (app) will give CC a sense of direction with common targets to work towards (1). Application could include: cookies; batch production; high - quality products; fresh ingredients; baking equipment; messages put on cookies; orders for birthdays or weddings; 10 production employees; 5 shop employees; Operations manager; oversee production; sole trader; small business; established for 5 years; sells at own shop in city centre . 8 0450 / 21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 11 of 20 Question Answer Marks

3(a) Explain two benefits and two limitations of CC using batch production. Award 1 mark for each relevant benefit/limitation (max 4). Award 1 additional mark for each explanation of the benefit/limitation applied to this context . Relevant benefits might include: â€¢ Flexible way of working â€“ can easily change to produce cookies for different special occasions â€¢ More variety in tasks than if flow production is used â€“ better motivation for the 10 production employees â€¢ More variety of products produced - to have personal messages written on them â€¢ Production may not be affected too much if baking equipment breaks down â€¢ Larger quantity produced than job production â€“ can keep restocking the shop with full variety of cookies â€¢ Raw materials can be purchased in larger quantities than job production so gain discounts / economies of scale / lower unit cost of fresh ingredients Relevant limitations might include: â€¢ Can be expensive / awkward to move part - finished goods around the factory â€“ t he business is only small â€¢ Machines have to be reset between batches â€“ takes time / delays production for the new Operations Manager â€¢ One mistake in production may mean the whole batch is affected / needs to be thrown away â€“ wasted ingredients â€¢ Warehouse space needed - for finished batches stored before being sold in CCâ€™s shop â€¢ Size of batch may be too small â€“ not enough cookies to meet demand / may be too large / left over cookies if not popular â€¢ May be more repetitive than job production â€“ 10 production workers may become bored For example: Flexible way of working (1) and can easily change the production process to produce different types of cookies (app). Application could include: cookies; sells at own shop; messages put on cookies; 10 production employees; 5 shop employees; Operations manager; fresh ingredients; special occasions; small business; sole trader; established for 5 years; competitorsâ€™ products sold in retail shops in other cities, personalised messages . 8 0450 / 21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 14 of 20 Question Answer Marks

1(a) Explain two advantages and two disadvantages of BB being a public limited company. Award 1 mark for each relevant advantage/disadvantage (max 4). Award 1 additional mark for each explanation of the advantage/disadvantage applied to this context . Relevant advantages might include: â€¢ Able to sell shares to the public â€“ so additional capital can be raised to finance the new factory â€¢ Rapid expansion possible by raising large sums of capital â€“ to become a multinational company â€¢ Limited liability would encourage more people to invest in the public limited company â€¢ Continuity Relevant disadvantages might include: â€¢ Accounts available to the public â€“ competing bike manufacturers can see the accounts â€¢ Pressure to pay large dividends to shareholders â€“ this restricts the planned expansion for the childrenâ€™s bike â€¢ Risk of takeover/original owners may lose control â€“ and use its flow production line to produce competitorsâ€™ products â€¢ Divorce between ownership and control â€¢ More complex accounting and reporting requirements/more legal formalities For example: Able to sell shares to the public (1) so additional capital can be raised to finance the new factory (app). Application could include: new factory; bikes; flow production; wants to become a multinational company; bike manufacturer; exports 30%; target market of adults and young children; components of bikes e.g. gears; 100 production workers; sells through specialist bike shops; high labour turnover; sales have increased every year since set up. 8 0450 / 22 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 7 of 21 Question Answer Marks

3(a) Explain one way each of the following stakeholder groups might be affected by a business becoming a multinational company. Award 1 mark for how each stakeholder group will be affected (max 2 marks for each stakeholder group). Award a maximum of 1 additional mark for each explanation of how the stakeholder group will be affected. There are no application marks available for this question. Effects on shareholders might include: â€¢ Value of assets of company increases â€“ as increased profit can be reinvested â€¢ Increased dividends â€“ profit increases from avoiding barriers to trade â€¢ Lower dividend s â€“ if need to fund expansion into other countries â€¢ Increased share price/value of shares increases â€“ as becomes a more well - known business due to market share increasing â€¢ Easier to sell shares â€“ as the company is known globally with higher revenue/profit/better image Effects on employees might include: â€¢ May lose jobs â€“ as business moves to produce in a country with lower labour/production costs â€¢ May gain promotion â€“ from business expanding into different markets / business can access a larger target market and additional jobs may be required â€¢ May have a more secure job â€“ as business remains competitive if competitor companies are also expanding abroad â€¢ Higher wages paid â€“ as business is more successful and can afford to increase wage rates Effects on suppliers: â€¢ May have increased sales â€“ as output of multinational increases â€¢ May lose sales â€“ as multinational may purchase from cheaper supplier s in other countries Effects on government: â€¢ Higher tax revenue if higher profit repatriated â€¢ May lose tax revenue if multinational locates head office in another country For example: Employees may lose their jobs (1) as the business becomes a multinational company and moves its production to another country to lower labour/production costs (1). 8 0450 / 22 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 15 of 21 Question Answer Marks

2(a) Explain two reasons why SA wants to keep labour turnover low. Award one mark for each reason (max 2). Award a maximum of 3 additional marks for each explanation of the reason why SA wants to keep labour turnover low â€“ one of which must be applied to this context. Reasons might include: â€¢ So recruitment costs will not be increased â€“ no need to pay to advertise a vacancy â€¢ Time is saved recruiting new employees â€“ no time is spent interviewing applicants â€“ so managers have more time to focus on other issues â€¢ Training costs will be kept lower â€“ as employees will already be trained â€¢ Keep s production at higher levels / less likely to disrupt output â€“ as employees do not leave so production is not interrupted by employees leaving â€“ orders can be filled without delays/meet demand â€¢ Has employees already trained if sales increase â€“ can quickly produce increased output from existing employees â€“ higher sales if can be amongst the first businesses to increase output if demand increases For example: Recruitment costs will not increase if labour turnover is kept low (1) so there is no need to pay to advertise a vacancy (1) to replace one of the 500 production employees (app) lowering cash outflows (1). Application could include: sustainable cotton; clothes; trading for 30 years; uses batch production; 500 production employees; JIT inventory control; high - quality products; forecast to move into the growth phase of the business cycle in 2025; experienced skilled workers. 8 0450 / 23 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 10 of 19 Question Answer Marks

4(a) Explain two advantages and two disadvantages of SA using just - in - time (JIT) inventory control. Award 1 mark for each relevant advantage/disadvantage (max 4). Award a maximum of 1 additional mark for each explanation of the advantage/disadvantage which must be applied to this context . Relevant advantages might include: â€¢ Reduces cost of holding inventory â€“ as fewer sustainable cotton raw materials will need to be stored â€¢ Warehouse space is not needed â€“ to store the part - finished clothes â€¢ Helps improve cash flow â€“ for the business that has been trading for 30 years â€¢ Less likely to have inventory that goes out of date/fashion Relevant disadvantages might include: â€¢ May not be able to meet an unexpected increase in demand â€“ as the economy comes out of the slump and goes into the growth stage of the business cycle in 2025 â€¢ There may be late deliveries of raw materials â€“ and the batch production process will be delayed â€¢ Relies on a good relationship with suppliers â€“ to maintain the brand image of protecting the environment â€¢ Cannot benefit from economies of scale â€¢ Less room for mistakes in the production process For example: Reduces the cost of holding inventory (1) as fewer sustainable cotton raw materials will be required to be stored ready for the production process (app). Application could include: sustainable cotton; clothes; trading for 30 years; high - quality products; uses batch production; 500 production employees; has its own shops; brand image for protecting the environment; forecast to move into the growth phase of the business cycle in 2025; low labour turnover; experienced skilled workers. 8 0450 / 23 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 17 of 19 Question Answer Marks

1(b) Relevant points might include: Paying bonuses â€¢ M ay be flexible in the way it is paid â€“ higher pay if the production team increase output of shoes â€“ ensuring that they each work harder so the team has an increased payment â€¢ May bring pay up to the same rate paid by local businesses for similar work â€“ employees less likely to leave â€“ lowering PFâ€™s recruitment costs â€¢ May cause disagreements if only some of the 20 employees are working hard â€“ may be seen as unfair if all get the same bonus while not all the employees work as hard as each other â€¢ Paying a bonus is likely to mean profits for the business are lower than expected â€“ may limit retained profit for expansion â€¢ Can become expected and if not paid then employees become demotivated â€“ reducing productivity Piece - rate â€¢ Wages paid to employees will increase if there is a higher output, so workers will be motivated to increase their output and produce more shoes in a shorter time â€“ increasing efficiency â€¢ If work is rushed to obtain more wages, then quality of the shoes might suffer â€“ leading to a poor reputation for the business â€“ fewer repeat customers â€“ lower revenue in the future â€¢ May be hard to calculate the contribution of each employee to the total number of shoes produced â€¢ It may be seen as unfair by employees if different jobs produce more output than others â€“ then motivation may reduce rather than increase 0450 / 21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 9 of 20 Question Answer Marks

1(b) Job rotation â€¢ Production workers carry out one task for a period of time and then swap round to carry out another task using natural rubber to make the job more interesting and less boring â€“ provides a greater variety of work â€¢ Makes rotating the workers around the new factory easier â€“ so can fill in for colleagues who are absent â€“ means less likely to have hold - ups in production â€“ output produced on time â€¢ May require training for employees which increases costs â€“ but once trained the employee should be more productive â€“ a one - off cost that will not keep being paid Recommendation Justification might include: â€¢ Paying a bonus is the best way to motivate the 20 employees as this will add extra to their wages for the work they do producing shoes and encourage them to work harder. Paying piece - rate may be difficult to calculate each week as employees do different jobs, and some may take longer than others. Job rotation may be difficult to introduce if the tasks are very different. â€¢ Paying workers using piece - rate is the best way to motivate employees as this means that employees who work harder producing shoes will be rewarded with higher wages which will make them feel it is all worth the effort, based on Taylorâ€™s theory of motivation. â€¢ Job rotation is the best way to motivate employees as this is the only one which makes the job of producing shoes more interesting and less boring . 2(b) Relevant points might include: Availability of raw materials â€¢ Natural rubber is available locally then this will reduce the transport costs â€“ reducing PFâ€™s costs â€¢ May make supplying extra raw materials at short notice easier â€“ especially if an urgent order is taken â€“ customer satisfaction more likely to be assured so customers may be more loyal â€“ maintaining a regular source of revenue â€¢ Forests are nearby then likely to have better relationship with suppliers â€“ may be more willing to give trade credit Transport links â€¢ If transport links are good, finished shoes are likely to reach the market more quickly â€“ reducing transport costs for deliveries â€¢ If transport links are good delivery vehicles will be less likely to be stuck in traffic/congestion which increases costs of paying the driver and the fuel for the vehicle â€“ quicker to access raw materials especially if PF uses JIT â€¢ May be easier for employees to get to work â€“ less likely to be late / arrive on time for shift â€“ more efficient production â€¢ May be able to attract employees from a wider area if transport links are good â€“ may be easier to recruit enough workers â€“ making it easier to start production at the new factory 0450 / 21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 13 of 20 Question Answer Marks

2(b) Government influence â€¢ Government grants may be available to businesses that locate in a particular area â€“ which reduces the costs of setting up the new factory in that area â€¢ Often offered in areas that have high levels of unemployment â€“ so there may be many people willing and able to work so easier to find employees for the new factory â€¢ Government grants may be given to help with training costs to produce shoes â€“ so training costs may not be as high as expected â€¢ There may be restrictions on the use of the land or emissions from the factory that may affect its operation Conclusion Justification might include: â€¢ Availability of natural rubber as without raw materials PF cannot produce any output. So , it is essential that raw materials are nearby so the factory can always operate. Transport links are less important if the market is nearby as delays will not cause major problems if the journey is short. Government influence may only be important at the set - up stage of the factory so will not be helpful in the long run. â€¢ Good transport links are essential as delays in deliveries of shoes to the market may lead to a decrease in customer loyalty. â€¢ Government influence is most important as grants can significantly reduce the set - up and training costs for employees to manufacture shoes making it more likely to be a success. 0450 / 21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 14 of 20 Question Answer Marks 3 ( a ) Explain two advantages and two disadvantages to a business of using a bank loan as a source of finance. 3(b) Relevant points might include: Advantages Disadvantages Leaflets handed out in the street â€¢ A cheap method of advertising shoes â€“ keep s marketing budget costs lower â€¢ Can be given out to a wide range of people â€“ may attract potential customers who may not otherwise see the advertisement â€¢ Can be kept for future reference â€“ so are more likely to remember the details of the rubber shoes â€“ and may therefore be more likely to make a purchase in the future â€¢ May not be read â€“ and just thrown away in the street â€“ wasted cost of producing the leaflets â€¢ May be seen as junk and can be annoying for potential customer of shoes â€“ so ignore it and not make a purchase â€¢ May lead to litter and cause pollution in the streets leading to a bad reputation for PF Billboards on the side of a main road â€¢ A permanent form of advertising for a period of time â€“ more likely to be seen more than once and therefore attract the attention of passers by â€¢ They can potentially be seen by a large number of people â€“ walking or driving past the billboard â€“ may attract the attention of many potential customers â€¢ Can easily be missed â€“ especially if the person is driving by and concentrating on the road/traffic â€¢ No detailed information about the sustainable sources of raw materials can be included in the billboard â€“ as only large writing can be seen, and so only brief general message can be on the billboard â€¢ Possibly higher cost of advertising compared to the other two for the shoe manufacturer 0450 / 21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 17 of 20 Question Answer Marks

This may mean that the business will have expanded too quickly and may have cash flow problems in the future â€¢ The low acid test ratio for 2026 suggests that the business is likely to have liquidity problems after the new factory is built â€¢ The expansion appears to have been forecast to be mainly funded by a large bank loan of $460 000 â€“ this may cause problems in the future as the interest repayments of this loan will result in a large increase in expenses â€“ possibly reducing profitability i n the future Recommendation Justification might include: â€¢ Philipâ€™s sister should invest in PF as the non - current assets are forecast to increase by nearly double and the building of the new factory is predicted to be financed mainly by a bank loan. The bank will be confident of a successful expansion of PF, or it will not grant such a big loan. This should help reassure Philipâ€™s sister that the investment will provide good returns in the future. â€¢ Philipâ€™s sister should not invest in PF as the forecast statement of financial position suggests that there may be significant liquidity problems producing shoes in the future with an expected fall in the current ratio of 0.05 and an expected fall in the acid test ratio of 0.4.

term debts â€“ if these debts cannot be paid back then the business may be illiquid and may have to cease trading â€¢ Measured using the current ratio to show the amount of current assets to current liabilities â€“ ratio should be about 1.5 to 2 to be at a satisfactory level so the business has up to twice as many current assets to current liabilities and can comfortably pa y its short - term debts such as to suppliers of ingredients â€¢ Can also be measured using the acid test ratio to show an ability of a business to pay its short - term debts not including inventory â€“ a measure around or just below 1 is a satisfactory level that shows that a business should be able to pay its short - term d ebts even if it cannot quickly turn inventories into cash â€¢ It can be used to indicate whether the business needs to hold less inventory if it has a low acid test ratio then it may be holding too m uch inventor y â€¢ It can also be used if Jemi needs to borrow as banks can assess the risk of lending to the vegan restaurant and whether it is likely to repay the loan / whether to grant the loan 0450 / 23 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 16 of 19 Question Answer Marks

mark_scheme_text: 4(b) Relevant points might include: Job enrichment â€¢ Workers in the restaurant to carry out additional tasks that require more skill or responsibility so makes the job less boring â€“ more customers served per hour â€“ reducing average costs â€¢ Workers may become more involved in decision - making in the restaurant â€“ makes them feel more valued by the business â€¢ May not want to take on additional responsibility â€“ which may demotivate employees â€“ leading to poorer customer service â€¢ May not have the skills to carry out the additional tasks â€“ additional training likely to be required â€“ increases costs Discount on meals in Jemiâ€™s restaurant â€¢ Encourages employee loyalty â€“ by giving the 12 employees the incentive to eat in the restaurant with family and friends â€“ increases sales â€¢ Likely to encourage lower labour turnover â€“ if employee values the discounts â€¢ Lower revenue after discount applied to meals â€“ reduces gross profit margin on meals served to employees â€¢ May be too low to have any impact / employee does not want to eat at the restaurant as does not like vegan food Recommendation Judgement might include: â€¢ Job enrichment should be chosen as the business is new and may not be able to afford to give a discount. It might be better to add additional tasks that require more responsibility to the job to try to make it more interesting and maintain increased motivation for a longer period of time. A discount may only motivate the 12 restaurant workers for a short time as they may get fed up with the meals at the restaurant. â€¢ Discounts on vegan meals in the restaurant will be the best way to motivate employees as it will save them and their families money when eating at the restaurant which will encourage loyalty.

4(e) Explain two methods of promotion a business might use for its products. Which method is likely to be the best for a large business to use? Justify your answer. Some analysis can be used for more than one method, but only award once. Analysis can be awarded for explaining: â€¢ Advantages of methods â€¢ Disadvantages of methods â€¢ How the method works 0450 / 11 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 27 of 27 Question Answer Marks Guidance 4 ( e ) Points might include: â€¢ Television [k] is expensive / can include moving images to attract attention [an] â€¢ Magazines [k] as able to target specific types of customers [an] â€¢ Discount offers / promotional pricing / ( money off) coupons / loyalty card [k] which can make the products more affordable [an] â€¢ Social media / internet - based advertising/influencers [k] to reach large numbers of people [an] â€¢ Leaflets [k] as this is a low - cost method [an] â€¢ Posters/billboards [k] as can be seen by everyone who is passing by [an] â€¢ Point of sale displays/demonstrations [k] as this allows the customer to be shown how it is used [an] â€¢ Radio advertising [k] which allows them to target specific regions [an] â€¢ Newspapers [k] â€¢ Gifts [k] â€¢ Sponsorship [k] â€¢ Competitions [k] encourage customer to buy to receive a reward/win [an] â€¢ Free samples [k] increase costs [an] â€¢ BOGOF [k] â€¢ After - sales service [k] Other appropriate responses should be credited. Justification might include: Television [k] as able to include moving images to attract attention [an]. Another method is posters [k] as it can be seen by everyone who is passing by [an]. Television is the best method as this is a large business so it should be able to afford the cost involved [eval] and is likely to reach far more people than a poster [eval]. 6

mark_scheme_text: 1(b) Relevant points might include: Financial â€¢ Raise finance more easily and cheaply â€“ as lower risk of not repaying debts due to larger size of business â€¢ Banks may offer lower interest rates â€“ lower costs of borrowing for the expansion of the large business than if it was a smaller business Purchasing â€¢ Bulk buy components â€“ discounts / cheaper prices for bulk purchases â€“ reduces average / unit cost of components â€¢ May be able to lower prices â€“ as average cost of each product is lower â€“ becomes more competitive â€“ increases sales of bathroom products â€“ revenue increases Managerial â€¢ Specialist managers can be employed â€“ better at their jobs / more knowledgeable as only carried out this task â€“ e.g. marketing manager knows best place to advertise to be more cost efficient â€¢ Able to attract the most talented managers â€“ more efficient in manufacturing luxury bathroom products Conclusion Justification might include: â€¢ Financial econom ies of scale will have the greatest effect as LB plans to expand and build a new factory in another country and will probably need to raise some of the finance by borrowing. This will keep down the repayment costs of expanding. LB is already producing on a large scale so the increased benefits from the purchasing and managerial economies may be limited leaving f inancial economies giving the biggest reduction in unit costs. â€¢ Purchasing econom ies of scale will have the greatest effect on LBâ€™s average costs as it produces bathroom products on a large scale so discounts on components will reduce unit costs by a significant amount, increasing gross profit and profit. â€¢ Managerial econom ies of scale will have the biggest effect on unit costs as all managers will be experienced in producing showers and baths and are specialists in their areas leading to efficiencies in all departments of the business. 0450 / 21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 9 of 20 Question Answer Marks 2 ( a ) Explain two advantages and two disadvantages to LB of using primary market research. mark_scheme_text: 4(b) Relevant points might include: Benefits Drawbacks â€¢ It creates jobs â€“ lowering unemployment in country Y â€“ raising incomes in the country and helping to increase economic growth/GDP â€¢ Increased investment â€“ the new factory for manufacturing bathroom products will mean new infrastructure may be built also benefiting local businesses â€¢ LB could export from country Y â€“ improving its balance of payments â€“ earns foreign currency for country Y to purchase more imports â€¢ LB may pay taxes to the government â€“ increases tax revenue to provide more services for its population â€¢ Increased consumer choice â€“ more competition and more luxury bathroom products available to buy â€“ as consumers have increasing incomes and it has increased demand for luxury products â€¢ The jobs created may be unskilled and low paid â€“ may make it more difficult for local businesses to meet their labour needs as they expand due to increasing economic growth â€¢ There may be reduced sales for the local bathroom manufacturers as their costs and prices may be higher â€¢ Profits may be sent back to country X â€“ loss of government tax revenue in country Y â€¢ Scarce resources may be used up quickly leaving few non - renewable resources remaining when LB leaves â€“ making economic growth in the future slow down â€¢ LB may have some political influence and gain financial support such as grants that could have been spent on local businesses â€¢ Increased noise and air pollution when the factory producing bathroom products is being built â€“ may cause damage to the environment and health of the local population Conclusion Justification might include: â€¢ Allowing LB to locate in the country has more benefits for country Y than it has drawbacks. The jobs created will help the country develop and may benefit from the transfer of new technology from LB to the many other bathroom producers in the country. This may increase their efficiency and improve their productivity. So many other local businesses may benefit which outweighs the drawbacks of LB causing pollution when building the new factory as this is only in the short term. â€¢ The government in country Y should not allow LB to build a factory there as it may not gain tax revenue if the government of country Y has to offer financial support to attract LB to locate there. LB may have lower unit costs from the economies of scale it benefits from as it is a large business and therefore may cause the many local bathroom manufacturing businesses to close if they cannot compete.

1(b) Relevant points might include: Advantages Disadvantages Telephone calls â€¢ Speaks directly to each supplier of ribbons â€“ gets immediate feedback â€“ so knows the message has been received â€¢ Can verify if the message for the order of fresh flowers is understood â€“ answer questions about the orders â€“ to ensure the correct flowers are delivered â€“ possibly meeting customer orders more quickly â€¢ Know the message has been received â€“ with having a good relationship with the supplier which means they will answer and take the order â€¢ Suppliers may not answer â€“ delay in order being taken â€“ delivery of flowers may be delayed â€“ which slows production and may be late being delivered to a wedding â€¢ Poor connection over the network used â€“ may distort the message and make it difficult to understand â€“ resulting in wrong order being made â€“ which may lead to under or over ordered flowers â€¢ Could take a long time to call suppliers â€“ may not answer straight away and may need to call several times taking time that could be used for something else â€¢ No permanent record of the conversation â€“ if an incorrect order is made there is no proof the supplier made a mistake 0450 / 22 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 9 of 22 Question Answer Marks

1(b) Advantages Disadvantages Emails â€¢ Provides a written record for FF to refer to â€“ if the suppliers do not meet the order correctly and the wrong flowers are delivered â€¢ Can be referred to at different times to check on the amount of perishable supplies ordered to ensure the correct order is delivered on the correct date â€¢ Kathryn does not have to be in the office to send the order â€“ can be sent out of usual office hours â€“ may be more convenient especially as FF is a small business â€¢ Some suppliers may not have access to a reliable internet connection â€“ so FF may not know the order for ribbons has not been received â€¢ The order may not be seen if there are too many emails to read and get lost or missed â€¢ May go into junk â€“ so supplier of packaging is unaware there is an order Recommendation Justification might include: â€¢ A telephone call should be made if the order for ribbons is needed quickly to ensure the message is received. Feedback can clarify that the correct order has been made. An email may get lost amongst many emails and the urgency may not be realised, therefor e making the order late being processed and delivery of the flower arrangements for the wedding being delayed. â€¢ An email should be used as it is sent straight away, and the supplier of fresh flowers can reply letting FF know that the order has been received and if it can be dispatched straight away. 0450 / 22 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 10 of 22 Question Answer Marks 2 ( a ) Explain two benefits and two limitations of a business operating in a niche market. 4(b) Advantages Disadvantages Using electricity from renewable sources â€¢ Savings of electricity after solar panels bought â€“ $100 per week saving on electricity for each shop â€¢ $5200 per year savings for each shop / $15 600 per year for all 3 shops â€¢ These cost savings will continue into the future â€“ after the installation costs are repaid â€¢ May be able to reduce prices as costs of power are reduced â€“ become more competitive â€¢ High cost to install the solar panels â€“ costs $6000 for each shop â€¢ $18 000 if all 3 shops have solar panels â€¢ May need to take out a loan â€“ interest costs will increase â€“ will increase cash outflow â€¢ Relies on amount of sunlight each day â€“ may be lower at different times of the year / difficult to store the electricity â€¢ Maintenance cost for the solar panels Recommendation Justification may include: â€¢ Kathryn should buy all FFâ€™s flowers from sustainable sources as FF may attract many more customers who are concerned about the environment , and they may be willing to pay a higher price if 25% increased cost of the flowers is passed on to customers. Purchasing solar panels may require taking out a bank loan and this will increase expenses which FF may not be able to afford given FF is opening a new shop and already has to find ways to finance its working capital. â€¢ Kathryn should use renewable energy to provide electricity for FFâ€™s shops. This is an investment that will continue to save $300 each week for the 3 shops in the future and may help to make FF more competitive

1(b) Relevant points might include: Advantages Disadvantages Partnership â€¢ It is easy to set - up â€“ as there are few legal requirements â€“ reducing the costs of setting up, such as accountancy costs â€¢ More capital can be invested â€“ as three partners investing their own money into the business â€¢ More owners to share responsibilities with and discuss business decisions â€“ each partner can specialise in different aspects of the business, such as one brother has experience of financial management â€“ more efficient as each partner uses their expertise â€¢ More new ideas as there are three partners â€“ so may make better decisions â€¢ Complete privacy â€“ they do not have to share any financial details with anyone else outside the business â€“ as has been the case for the last 4 years - apart from the tax office, as they will still have to pay tax â€¢ All three owners are motivated to work hard as it is their business and no one else benefits from their efforts â€¢ Unlimited liability / no limited liability â€“ which means all their personal possessions are at risk â€“ not just the money they invested in the business â€¢ No continuity as the business and the owners do not have a separate legal identity â€“ if one of the partners leaves, because the partners are â€˜the businessâ€™ it will end â€¢ Partners may disagree on business decisions, and this may make decision - making slower â€¢ If one of the partners doesnâ€™t work hard or is dishonest then the other two partners will also have responsibility for their actions 0450 / 23 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 9 of 20 Question Answer Marks

1(b) Advantages Disadvantages Private limited company â€¢ The three brothers and the business have a separate legal identity â€“ company can be sued instead of the owners â€¢ The business can sell shares to family and friends to raise more capital for the boat business â€“ larger amounts of capital may be raised than if a partnership â€¢ Limited liability â€“ will not lose personal possessions if business fails â€¢ Cannot be taken over without the consent of the shareholders â€¢ Accounts for the boat manufacturer are not private â€“ competitors may access the accounts â€¢ Not easy to transfer shares so may make it more difficult for the business to sell shares to family and friends â€¢ Legal formalities to operate the company â€“ needs to have accounts audited and registered with the government takes time and increases costs for ES â€¢ Legal formalities setting up the company â€“ ha ve to complete paperwork to register the company which takes time and money - may take more time for ES than setting up as a partnership Recommendation Justification might include: â€¢ The business should be set up as a partnership because the brothers will have complete control of which boats to produce and will be able to keep all the profit from their hard work without having to share it if they had other shareholders.
